prompts: On the third day of a sprint, the team finds a critical bug in the increment delivered by the previous sprint. How should this be handled in a Scrum context?
Mid-sprint, testers find a critical defect in last sprint's delivered increment. In Scrum, how should the team handle it?
Three days into the sprint, a severe bug surfaces in work already shipped last sprint. What is the Scrum-aligned response?
A critical production bug from the prior sprint appears on day three of the current sprint. How should Scrum guide handling it?

correct.text: Log the bug in the product backlog and fix it according to its prioritization.
correct.why: New work goes through the backlog so the product owner can prioritize it.

correct.text: Add the defect to the product backlog and address it based on product owner priority.
correct.why: Backlog prioritization keeps order transparent against other value.

correct.text: Place the bug on the product backlog so it can be ordered and pulled when prioritized.
correct.why: Scrum routes new work via the backlog rather than ad-hoc disruption.

distractors.text: Ignore the bug until the current sprint ends and address it afterward.
distractors.trap: Lets defects accumulate and degrade the product.

distractors.text: Require the testing team to fix it, since they missed it during their process.
distractors.trap: Blames a role; quality is a whole-team responsibility.

distractors.text: Immediately halt the current sprint to fix the bug right away.
distractors.trap: Overreacts and disrupts the sprint goal unnecessarily.

distractors.text: Create a separate defect queue owned only by developers, outside the backlog.
distractors.trap: Bypasses product owner ordering and transparency.

distractors.text: Extend the current sprint length until the critical bug is fully resolved.
distractors.trap: Breaks the timebox; confuses planning and velocity.

distractors.text: Wait for a formal change board approval before any defect work can start.
distractors.trap: Heavyweight gate delays agile defect handling.

explanation: Delivery performance domain: new work, including defects, enters the product backlog for prioritized handling rather than disrupting the timebox.

prompts: A team using iterative development struggles to keep pace as customer needs keep evolving. A retrospective reveals the feedback loop from testing to integration and back to development is slow, causing delays. What is the most effective action to improve efficiency and adaptability?

An iterative product team cannot keep up with changing customer needs. Retrospectives show testing-to-integration-to-development feedback is too slow. What action best improves efficiency and adaptability?
On a fintech delivery team, evolving requirements outpace the group. Slow handoffs from test through integration back to code are the bottleneck. What is the most effective fix?
Customer needs change faster than the team can respond. The feedback path from testing into integration and development is lagging. How should the team improve?

correct.text: Introduce CI/CD practices to streamline the feedback and deployment cycles.
correct.why: Automated build, test, and deploy shortens loops and surfaces issues early.

correct.text: Adopt continuous integration and delivery to speed feedback and release cycles.
correct.why: CI/CD tightens the test-to-dev loop for faster adaptation.

correct.text: Implement CI/CD automation so build, test, and deploy feedback arrives faster.
correct.why: Automation compresses feedback and supports iterative responsiveness.

distractors.text: Adopt a waterfall model to gain tighter control over the development process.
distractors.trap: Sacrifices the adaptability iterative delivery depends on.

distractors.text: Lengthen each iteration to allow more testing and integration time.
distractors.trap: Slows cadence without fixing the slow feedback loop.

distractors.text: Freeze requirements until the next cycle to keep development stable.
distractors.trap: Blocks responsiveness to evolving customer needs.

distractors.text: Add more manual review gates before any change reaches integration.
distractors.trap: Lengthens the loop instead of streamlining it.

distractors.text: Batch all testing into a dedicated phase after several iterations complete.
distractors.trap: Defers feedback; worsens late discovery of issues.

distractors.text: Reduce automated tests so developers can spend more time coding features.
distractors.trap: Cuts feedback quality; delays detection of defects.

explanation: Delivery performance domain / value delivery: fast feedback and continuous integration enable rapid, incremental adaptation to change.

prompts: In a story workshop a stakeholder proposes a large story: 'As a marketing analyst, I want to generate campaign reports with customizable metrics, automated scheduling, and multi-format export.' The team estimates 21 points, but sprint velocity is 15. The product owner wants it in the next sprint. What should you recommend?

A stakeholder offers a big story for campaign reports with custom metrics, scheduling, and multi-format export. Estimate is 21 points against velocity of 15. The PO wants it next sprint. What do you recommend?

During refinement, marketing wants one story covering custom metrics, auto-scheduling, and multi-format export for campaign reports—21 points, velocity 15. The product owner still targets the next sprint. What should you advise?
A 21-point story combining customizable campaign metrics, automated scheduling, and multi-format export exceeds the team's 15-point velocity. The PO wants it next sprint. What is your recommendation?

correct.text: Work with the team to split the story into smaller sprint-sized slices that each deliver value.
correct.why: Splitting keeps items small and delivers usable value within velocity.

correct.text: Collaborate to decompose the story into smaller valuable pieces that fit a sprint.
correct.why: Decomposition honors INVEST size and matches team capacity.

correct.text: Break the large story into thinner, value-bearing slices sized for the sprint velocity.
correct.why: Smaller slices fit capacity and still deliver incremental value.

distractors.text: Negotiate a longer sprint duration so the full story fits in one iteration.
distractors.trap: Breaks the fixed timebox and distorts velocity.

distractors.text: Run a technical spike now, then re-estimate the whole story for the next sprint.
distractors.trap: Spikes reduce uncertainty, not size; still oversized.

distractors.text: Add extra developers temporarily to raise velocity enough to fit the story.
distractors.trap: Adding people rarely boosts near-term output.

distractors.text: Commit to the full 21-point story and carry unfinished work into the next sprint.
distractors.trap: Overcommits; weakens forecasting and goal focus.

distractors.text: Cut acceptance criteria until the estimate drops below velocity without real splits.
distractors.trap: Hides scope; does not create valuable thin slices.

distractors.text: Ask the stakeholder to accept lower quality so the team can finish all parts faster.
distractors.trap: Sacrifices quality; still leaves a too-large item.

explanation: Delivery performance domain: decompose work into small, valuable increments that fit the team's capacity.

topic: Adaptive planning: adopt new tech + training
prompts: Halfway through a data-mining project you discover more efficient algorithms that could shorten the timeline and improve results, but they require extra team training. As project manager, how should you handle this?
Midway on an analytics project, better algorithms appear that may cut time and improve outcomes, yet training is required. How should you proceed as PM?
On a research data project, you learn of more efficient algorithms that could speed delivery and quality, with a training cost. What is the adaptive response?
Halfway through delivery, new algorithms promise shorter timelines and better results but need team upskilling. How should the project manager handle this?

correct.text: Weigh benefits and costs; if worthwhile, update the plan for training and inform stakeholders.
correct.why: Adaptive planning evaluates change, adjusts the plan, and informs stakeholders.

correct.text: Assess value versus training cost, revise the plan if justified, and communicate with stakeholders.
correct.why: Evaluate, replan, and keep stakeholders transparent about the change.

correct.text: Analyze trade-offs; if benefits outweigh costs, add training and notify stakeholders.
correct.why: Benefit-cost analysis plus transparent replanning is adaptive practice.

distractors.text: Adopt the new algorithms immediately without accounting for the training the team will need.
distractors.trap: Skips analysis; unfamiliarity causes delays.

distractors.text: Add the new algorithms into delivery but do not update stakeholders about the change.
distractors.trap: Poor transparency breeds confusion.

distractors.text: Ignore the algorithms entirely and stick strictly to the original approved plan.
distractors.trap: Misses value by refusing to adapt.

distractors.text: Train the team quietly after hours without changing the baseline schedule or budget.
distractors.trap: Hides cost and risk; skips formal replanning.

distractors.text: Switch algorithms first, then schedule training only if performance problems appear later.
distractors.trap: Reactive; training should be planned upfront.

distractors.text: Defer any decision until project close so the next phase can evaluate the tools instead.
distractors.trap: Delays potential value for the current effort.

explanation: Planning performance domain: planning is iterative, so evaluate beneficial changes, tailor the plan, and communicate with stakeholders.

topic: Responding to change: reprioritize with client
prompts: During a sprint review the client wants to shift the project's focus to address emerging market trends, which could alter the original scope. Following the Agile Manifesto, how do you facilitate the team's response?
In a sprint review, the customer asks to redirect effort toward new market trends that may change scope. Per the Agile Manifesto, how should you guide the team?
A client at review proposes a focus shift for emerging market needs that could revise scope. How do you facilitate the response in line with Agile values?
During review, the client seeks a direction change for new market trends, potentially altering scope. How should you help the team respond?

correct.text: Collaborate with the client to reassess priorities and integrate the changes where feasible.
correct.why: Welcoming change for customer advantage keeps the product valuable.

correct.text: Work with the client to re-evaluate priorities and fold in feasible changes for greater value.
correct.why: Customer collaboration and adaptive prioritization maximize relevance.

correct.text: Partner with the client to reprioritize and incorporate practical changes into the plan.
correct.why: Joint reprioritization embraces change that benefits the customer.

distractors.text: Have the team integrate the changes only after the current sprint, to protect velocity.
distractors.trap: Treats plan adherence as more important than value.

distractors.text: Keep the team on the predefined scope until the sprint is complete.
distractors.trap: Ignores emerging, higher-value needs.

distractors.text: Tell the client changes can only be implemented in later development cycles.
distractors.trap: Defers valuable change unnecessarily.

distractors.text: Require a formal change control board before discussing any new priorities.
distractors.trap: Heavy process delays responsive customer collaboration.

distractors.text: Reject the request to preserve the baseline scope and original business case.
distractors.trap: Resists change that could increase customer value.

distractors.text: Ask the team to estimate everything first, then decide without the client present.
distractors.trap: Excludes the customer from priority decisions.

explanation: Principle of embracing adaptability and change: respond to change over rigidly following a plan to maximize value.

topic: Adaptive replanning after supplier can no longer deliver a critical component
prompts: Halfway through a product-development project, your key supplier says they can no longer provide a component essential to success. Applying adaptive planning, how should you proceed?
Mid-project, a critical supplier can no longer deliver an essential component. Using adaptive planning, what should you do?
On a product build, your primary vendor stops supplying a component the product depends on. How do you proceed with adaptive planning?
Halfway through development, the key supplier withdraws a component vital to success. What is the adaptive planning response?

correct.text: Work with the team to find alternative solutions or suppliers, adjust the plan, and inform stakeholders.
correct.why: Respond to disruption by exploring options, replanning, and informing stakeholders.

correct.text: Collaborate to identify substitute solutions or vendors, revise the plan, and update stakeholders.
correct.why: Adaptive planning seeks alternatives, updates the plan, and stays transparent.

correct.text: Explore other suppliers or approaches with the team, replan accordingly, and communicate to stakeholders.
correct.why: Options plus replanning and communication handle supplier loss adaptively.

distractors.text: Cancel the project since the component is unavailable and notify stakeholders of termination.
distractors.trap: Gives up before exploring alternatives.

distractors.text: Delay any changes and work low-priority tasks while awaiting the supplier's resolution.
distractors.trap: Passive waiting stalls progress.

distractors.text: Keep following the original plan, assuming the supplier will resolve it soon.
distractors.trap: Ignores the risk with no contingency.

distractors.text: Hide the supplier issue until a replacement is signed to avoid worrying stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Withholds risk information stakeholders need.

distractors.text: Continue only the work that does not use the component and leave the plan unchanged.
distractors.trap: Avoids replanning the critical path dependency.

distractors.text: Transfer full responsibility to procurement and pause the project team until they finish.
distractors.trap: Idles the team; PM still owns adaptive response.

explanation: Planning performance domain: adapt the plan proactively to obstacles and communicate changes to manage expectations.

topic: Collaborative feature prioritization by value when team and stakeholders conflict
prompts: As product owner you find the team split over two features: developers prefer Feature A for its technical challenge, while stakeholders push Feature B for its market impact. What is your best course of action?
Developers favor Feature A for technical interest; stakeholders insist on Feature B for market value. As product owner, what should you do?
The team divides between Feature A (engineering appeal) and Feature B (stakeholder market impact). As PO, what is your best action?
As product owner, engineers want Feature A for its technical challenge and stakeholders want Feature B for market impact. How should you proceed?

correct.text: Facilitate a discussion of benefits and risks so team and stakeholders decide together.
correct.why: Collaborative, value-based prioritization balances technical and business views.

correct.text: Lead a joint session on benefits and risks of both features so the group decides.
correct.why: Shared value analysis yields an informed priority decision.

correct.text: Bring team and stakeholders together to compare benefits and risks collaboratively.
correct.why: Facilitated dialogue balances perspectives for better value choices.

distractors.text: Make a unilateral call based solely on your own preference for Feature A or B.
distractors.trap: Ignores team and stakeholder input.

distractors.text: Split team resources evenly across both features and pursue them at the same time.
distractors.trap: Overstretches capacity and delays value.

distractors.text: Choose Feature B automatically because stakeholders always have the final say.
distractors.trap: Prioritizes influence over actual value.

distractors.text: Choose Feature A to keep developer motivation and technical growth as the top goal.
distractors.trap: Favors interest over product value.

distractors.text: Defer both features and fill the sprint with uncontroversial technical debt work only.
distractors.trap: Avoids the value decision stakeholders need.

distractors.text: Let the highest-ranking stakeholder pick without any benefits-and-risks discussion.
distractors.trap: Skips collaborative value analysis.

explanation: Delivery performance domain: prioritize by value through collaboration, balancing business and technical considerations.

topic: Improving poorly-defined backlog items via coaching and refinement
prompts: As a servant leader you see the team repeatedly miss sprint goals because backlog items are poorly defined and hard to estimate. How should you handle this?
Sprint goals keep slipping because items are vague and estimates are unreliable. As a servant leader, how should you respond?
The team misses sprint goals again; backlog items lack clarity and are hard to size. How should a servant leader address this?
Poorly defined backlog items make estimation hard and sprint goals fail often. What should you do as a servant leader?

correct.text: Coach clear backlog writing and strengthen refinement to improve understanding and estimates.
correct.why: Better item quality and refinement fix the root cause of missed goals.

correct.text: Teach clearer backlog writing and improve refinement so items are understood and estimable.
correct.why: Coaching plus stronger refinement targets definition quality directly.

correct.text: Help the team craft well-defined items and deepen refinement for better shared estimates.
correct.why: Clarity and refinement remove the cause of missed sprint goals.

distractors.text: Implement a consequence framework that penalizes the team when sprint goals are not met.
distractors.trap: Punishes symptoms; ignores definition quality.

distractors.text: Reduce the scope of each backlog item at sprint start so they always fit capacity.
distractors.trap: Masks poor definitions; may cut value.

distractors.text: Ask members to work extra hours near sprint end to close gaps and still hit the goal.
distractors.trap: Unsustainable; does not fix poor item definitions.

distractors.text: Lower the definition of done so incomplete items can still count toward the sprint goal.
distractors.trap: Hides quality issues; weakens delivery standards.

distractors.text: Have the product owner write all estimates alone to speed planning and avoid debate.
distractors.trap: Removes team ownership of estimates and understanding.

distractors.text: Skip refinement entirely and start coding sooner so more sprint time remains available.
distractors.trap: Worsens ambiguity that already causes misses.

explanation: Delivery performance domain / servant leadership: improve backlog quality and coach the team rather than coerce output.

topic: TDD cycle: refactor and verify all tests after green
prompts: In Test-Driven Development, a developer wants to ensure new code meets requirements without breaking existing features. After writing the initial failing test and making it pass, what should they do next?
Using TDD, a developer has written a failing test and then made it pass. What is the next step to keep quality without regressions?
In a TDD cycle, the red test is green after minimal implementation. What should the developer do next?
After the failing test passes in TDD, what is the correct next action to protect existing behavior?

correct.text: Refactor the code and confirm all tests still pass.
correct.why: Refactor cleans structure; the full suite guards against regressions.

correct.text: Improve the code's structure, then re-run the full test suite to ensure it stays green.
correct.why: Refactor-after-green with all tests prevents technical debt and breakage.

correct.text: Clean up the implementation through refactoring while verifying every test still passes.
correct.why: TDD's next step is refactor with continuous suite verification.

distractors.text: Run a full system test to check for integration issues.
distractors.trap: Integration testing comes later, not in the TDD loop.

distractors.text: Swap the new test for an older one to confirm compatibility.
distractors.trap: Tests aren't exchanged; all are kept and run.

distractors.text: Focus only on the newly added tests to confirm they pass.
distractors.trap: Ignores possible regressions in existing behavior.

distractors.text: Delete the temporary tests used during the red phase to reduce suite noise.
distractors.trap: Keeps tests; they document and protect behavior.

distractors.text: Merge immediately and schedule refactoring for a later technical story.
distractors.trap: Defers the built-in refactor step of TDD.

distractors.text: Write more production features first, then circle back to improve structure.
distractors.trap: Skips refactor while the context is still fresh.

explanation: Delivery/quality performance domain: TDD's red-green-refactor builds in quality, verifying the whole suite after each change.

topic: Deliver working software early for feedback
prompts: An agile team has built a new feature and the customer is eager to see it, but some members want to wait until it's polished. Per the Agile Manifesto, what should the team prioritize?
A new feature is ready enough to show, and the customer wants a look, yet some teammates prefer waiting for polish. What should the team prioritize under Agile values?
Customers want an early look at a new feature; a few team members prefer delaying until it is fully polished. Per the Agile Manifesto, what takes priority?
The team can demo working software now, but some urge waiting for more polish first. What should agile practice prioritize?

correct.text: Give an early demo of the working software to gather customer feedback.
correct.why: Early working software drives collaboration and lets the team adapt.

correct.text: Demonstrate the working feature early so the customer can provide feedback.
correct.why: Continuous delivery of working software enables fast learning.

correct.text: Show the customer the working software now to collect feedback and adjust.
correct.why: Early demos beat delayed polish for learning and value.

distractors.text: Delay the demo until the feature is fully polished and passes all internal QA.
distractors.trap: Withholds working software and delays feedback.

distractors.text: Focus on comprehensive documentation before any customer demo.
distractors.trap: Values documentation over working software.

distractors.text: Ensure the process is strictly followed before showing anything to the customer.
distractors.trap: Prioritizes process over customer interaction.

distractors.text: Wait for a formal UAT phase after the full release train finishes.
distractors.trap: Defers feedback until late in delivery.

distractors.text: Polish the UI exclusively first, then schedule a demo only if time remains.
distractors.trap: Favors cosmetic polish over early customer learning.

distractors.text: Send status slides instead of a live demo until everything is production-ready.
distractors.trap: Reports replace working software the customer needs to see.

explanation: Value delivery principle: working software and customer collaboration take precedence, enabling rapid feedback.

topic: Adjust sprint scope for reduced capacity
prompts: A key developer tells the team they'll be on leave for two weeks of the next sprint. The product owner, worried about the release deadline, insists all planned work still be completed. As agile coach, what should you do?
A critical engineer will miss two weeks of the next sprint. The PO still wants the full planned backlog finished for a release date. As agile coach, what should you do?
Next sprint loses a key developer for two weeks, but the product owner demands no cut to planned work. How should you respond as agile coach?
With a core developer on leave for two weeks of the coming sprint, the PO insists the original plan still ship. What should the agile coach do?

correct.text: Facilitate a discussion with the team and product owner to adjust the backlog for reduced capacity.
correct.why: Replanning to real capacity sets honest expectations and protects pace.

correct.text: Help the team and product owner replan the sprint backlog to match lower available capacity.
correct.why: Honest capacity-based planning avoids overcommitment and burnout.

correct.text: Guide a joint conversation to resize sprint scope given the developer's two-week absence.
correct.why: Scope must fit capacity; facilitate transparent replanning.

distractors.text: Reassign the absent developer's tasks to others without changing the sprint goal at all.
distractors.trap: Overloads the team and ignores real capacity.

distractors.text: Ask the absent developer to work remotely during their leave to still hit the deadline.
distractors.trap: Disrespects the person and isn't sustainable.

distractors.text: Encourage the team to work extra hours so that all of the planned work is completed.
distractors.trap: Overtime over honest replanning risks burnout.

distractors.text: Cancel the developer's leave so the original sprint commitment can stand unchanged.
distractors.trap: Violates wellbeing; not a sustainable agile practice.

distractors.text: Keep the full backlog and lower quality expectations to finish everything on the deadline.
distractors.trap: Sacrifices quality instead of adjusting scope.

distractors.text: Promise the release date unchanged and defer capacity discussion until the end of the sprint.
distractors.trap: Avoids timely replanning when capacity is already known.

explanation: Team/delivery performance domain: plan to actual capacity and maintain a sustainable pace through transparent collaboration.

prompts: A team practicing TDD finishes the green phase with minimal code that passes the test but has duplicate logic and unclear variable names. Per TDD principles, what should guide their next move?
After TDD's green phase, code passes tests yet has duplication and unclear names. What should guide the next step under TDD?
TDD just made the test pass with bare-minimum code that is messy and duplicated. What principle should drive the team's next move?
Minimal green code works but shows duplicate logic and poor naming. According to TDD, what should come next?

correct.text: The refactor phase should happen now, improving structure while keeping all tests passing.
correct.why: Refactoring right after green is a disciplined TDD step against debt.

correct.text: Refactor immediately to clean structure while ensuring the full suite remains green.
correct.why: Immediate refactor after green prevents technical debt buildup.

correct.text: Move into refactor now: improve design and naming without breaking any tests.
correct.why: TDD builds refactor into the cycle right after green.

distractors.text: Whether to refactor depends on stakeholders authorizing time for code improvement.
distractors.trap: Refactoring is intrinsic to TDD, not externally approved.

distractors.text: Refactoring should wait until several features accumulate technical debt to clean up.
distractors.trap: Deferring defeats the purpose of the cycle.

distractors.text: Refactoring should wait for sprint review to check alignment with business priorities.
distractors.trap: Delays quality work past its proper moment.

distractors.text: Skip refactor if velocity is tight and schedule cleanup only after the release.
distractors.trap: Postpones the built-in third step of TDD.

distractors.text: Write the next failing test first and leave the messy code as is for now.
distractors.trap: Skips refactor while context and tests are ready.

distractors.text: Ask a code-review board to approve a separate refactoring project first.
distractors.trap: Over-formalizes a normal step of the TDD loop.

explanation: Quality/delivery performance domain: TDD's red-green-refactor makes refactoring an immediate, disciplined part of the cycle.

topic: PO reorders backlog on new market data
prompts: In Sprint 2 of a fintech product, market analytics reveal unexpected user behavior that sharply changes revenue forecasts. The product owner has already shared a draft release roadmap and must decide before Sprint 3, while the team asks about shifting priorities. Which action best reflects the product owner's duty to maximize value?

During Sprint 2, new market data shows user behavior that revises revenue forecasts. The PO shared a draft roadmap and must act before Sprint 3 as the team questions priorities. What best maximizes value?

Fresh analytics in Sprint 2 alter expected revenue for a fintech product. A draft release plan exists; Sprint 3 is near and priorities are in question. What should the product owner do to maximize value?
Unexpected user-behavior metrics in Sprint 2 change revenue forecasts. The product owner must respond before Sprint 3 with a shared draft roadmap already out. Which action best maximizes value?

correct.text: Reorder the backlog using the fresh metrics and share the revised plan with stakeholders at refinement.
correct.why: The PO maximizes value by applying new insight and communicating priorities.

correct.text: Update backlog order from the new data and communicate the revised plan in refinement.
correct.why: Value-driven reordering plus transparent communication is the PO's duty.

correct.text: Reprioritize the backlog with the latest metrics and present the updated plan to stakeholders.
correct.why: New insight must drive order, with stakeholders kept in the loop.

distractors.text: Freeze the backlog until quarterly planning and require formal change requests.
distractors.trap: Ignores high-impact data and delays value.

distractors.text: Promote only the most influential stakeholder's preferred features and defer the rest.
distractors.trap: Serves influence, not overall product value.

distractors.text: Order a feasibility study while telling the team to keep building the original commitments.
distractors.trap: Treats commitments as fixed, delaying adaptation.

distractors.text: Keep the draft roadmap fixed so stakeholders are not confused by changes.
distractors.trap: Stability over value wastes the new insight.

distractors.text: Let the development team reprioritize items based on technical risk alone.
distractors.trap: Value ordering remains the product owner's accountability.

distractors.text: Wait until Sprint 3 retrospective to discuss whether forecasts matter to scope.
distractors.trap: Delays action past the decision window given.

explanation: Value delivery performance domain: the product owner maximizes value by reprioritizing on new evidence and communicating changes.

topic: User story benefit clause for prioritization
prompts: Refining the backlog, a team hits a story: 'As a customer service representative, I want to access customer purchase history.' They realize it's missing an element that would help prioritize it against other items. Which missing component best communicates the business justification?
A backlog item reads: 'As a customer service representative, I want to access customer purchase history.' Something needed for prioritization is missing. What best conveys business justification?
During refinement, the story 'As a customer service rep, I want to access purchase history' lacks an element that supports prioritization. Which component best states business justification?

The team reviews 'As a customer service representative, I want to access customer purchase history' and notes a gap for comparing value to other items. What missing piece best communicates justification?

correct.text: A benefit statement explaining why the rep needs the access and what value it provides.
correct.why: The 'so that' clause conveys business value for prioritization.

correct.text: A 'so that' benefit clause that states the value and reason for the capability.
correct.why: Benefit language supplies the business why used in prioritization.

correct.text: An outcome-focused benefit phrase describing the value of having purchase history access.
correct.why: Value wording enables comparing this story to others by benefit.

distractors.text: Acceptance criteria listing test scenarios and edge cases for retrieving purchase history.
distractors.trap: Defines 'done,' not the business justification.

distractors.text: Estimation points indicating the relative effort to complete the feature.
distractors.trap: Shows effort, not value.

distractors.text: Technical details describing how the system retrieves and displays the data.
distractors.trap: The 'how' isn't part of the story's value.

distractors.text: A dependency list of APIs and systems the story will touch during build.
distractors.trap: Dependencies aid planning, not business justification.

distractors.text: A UI mockup showing layout of the purchase history screen.
distractors.trap: Design aids understanding, not the value why.

distractors.text: A risk register entry noting possible data-privacy exposure.
distractors.trap: Risk matters but is not the story's benefit clause.

explanation: Scope/delivery performance domain: a user story's benefit ('so that') clause expresses value, driving prioritization.

prompts: On an ML dashboard project, two developers report sporadic failures in a multithreaded parser during the TDD cycle, clouding next-sprint commitments. As project manager keeping strict red-green-refactor discipline, which action best upholds TDD while addressing the concurrency bug?
A multithreaded parser fails intermittently on an ML dashboard; commitments for next sprint are unclear. Upholding red-green-refactor, how should you address the concurrency bug?
Sporadic race failures hit a multithreaded parser mid-TDD on a machine-learning UI project. As PM enforcing TDD discipline, what best handles the bug?
Two developers see intermittent multithreaded parser failures that threaten upcoming commitments. Strict TDD is required. Which action best addresses the concurrency issue?

correct.text: Write the smallest failing test that reproduces the race, make it pass, then refactor.
correct.why: A failing test keeps red-green-refactor intact and guards regressions.

correct.text: Create a minimal red test for the race condition, implement until green, then refactor.
correct.why: Test-first preserves TDD order while documenting the concurrency bug.

correct.text: Start with a tiny failing test that exposes the race, fix to green, then clean the design.
correct.why: Red-first keeps discipline and locks the fix against future races.

distractors.text: Fix the parser first, then write an encompassing regression test to confirm the fix.
distractors.trap: Reverses TDD order; changes go untested first.

distractors.text: Schedule a spike to benchmark threading libraries and delay writing further tests.
distractors.trap: Abandons test-first discipline for architecture work.

distractors.text: Skip unit tests and rely on nightly stress tests to catch the race.
distractors.trap: Drops unit-level, test-first feedback entirely.

distractors.text: Disable the flaky parser tests until after the next release stabilizes.
distractors.trap: Hides the bug instead of driving a red-first fix.

distractors.text: Assign a senior developer to rewrite the parser without adding new tests yet.
distractors.trap: Big rewrite without failing tests breaks TDD.

distractors.text: Document the race in the risk log and continue features until capacity frees up.
distractors.trap: Defers test-first handling of a known defect.

explanation: Quality/delivery performance domain: TDD requires a failing test first (red), then green, then refactor, even for concurrency bugs.

prompts: On a large software project, the work has fallen well behind due to unforeseen technical challenges, and anxious stakeholders are pressing for immediate progress. As product owner, which action is most appropriate?
Technical challenges have put a large software effort far behind schedule, and stakeholders demand visible progress. As product owner, what should you do?
Delivery is late after unexpected technical issues, and stakeholders are anxious for results. As product owner, which action is most appropriate?
A major product is behind after unforeseen technical hurdles, with stakeholders pushing for quick wins. What is the product owner's best response?

correct.text: Reprioritize toward high-value features that can ship quickly and rebuild stakeholder confidence.
correct.why: Quick, high-value increments show progress and restore trust.

correct.text: Reorder work to favor valuable items that can be delivered soon and rebuild confidence.
correct.why: Value-first quick delivery demonstrates progress under pressure.

correct.text: Focus the backlog on high-value, near-term deliverables that restore stakeholder trust.
correct.why: Shipping valuable increments soon addresses anxiety and adapts.

distractors.text: Stick strictly to the original plan to avoid confusion and still meet initial objectives.
distractors.trap: Fails to adapt and further erodes confidence.

distractors.text: Request more resources from stakeholders so the team can accelerate all remaining work.
distractors.trap: Slow to approve and doesn't fix root causes.

distractors.text: Delegate backlog prioritization to the development team to speed up decision-making.
distractors.trap: Prioritization by value is the product owner's accountability.

distractors.text: Hide the delay from stakeholders until a full recovery plan has been completely detailed.
distractors.trap: Reduces transparency when trust is already strained.

distractors.text: Pause all feature work and only produce status reports until stakeholder confidence returns.
distractors.trap: Reports without value delivery do not rebuild trust.

distractors.text: Commit to the full original scope with overtime so that nothing needs to be deprioritized.
distractors.trap: Unsustainable; ignores adaptive value prioritization.

explanation: Value delivery performance domain: adapt and reprioritize toward the highest value when under pressure.

prompts: A healthcare software team using iterative development finds that stakeholder demos generate lots of feedback, but only about a third gets addressed, with the rest deprioritized behind new feature requests. As project manager, what best improves feedback incorporation while keeping iteration momentum?
On a fintech app team, demos produce abundant stakeholder feedback, yet most of it loses out to new feature requests. How can you best weave feedback into planning without slowing iterations?
A retail e-commerce squad sees demo feedback pile up while only a minority is acted on; new requests keep winning priority. What approach best improves feedback handling and preserves delivery pace?
Stakeholder demos on your iterative product yield lots of feedback, but roughly two-thirds never get into planned work. As PM, how do you improve incorporation without adding heavy process?

correct.text: Triage feedback with the product owner during refinement, ranking it with new requests
correct.why: Feedback competes fairly with new work inside existing refinement, without extra overhead.

correct.text: With the product owner, categorize feedback in refinement alongside new work
correct.why: Single backlog and PO judgment keep momentum while feedback is fairly ranked.

correct.text: Run refinement triage with the product owner so feedback is prioritized with new items
correct.why: Integrates feedback into the same prioritization process without parallel systems.

distractors.text: Hold weekly meetings with all stakeholders to rank every feedback item before committing
distractors.trap: Heavy meeting overhead risks analysis paralysis.

distractors.text: Reserve a fixed share of each iteration capacity solely for stakeholder feedback items
distractors.trap: Too rigid; ignores that feedback value varies.

distractors.text: Create a separate feedback backlog that stakeholders manage and vote on themselves
distractors.trap: Fragments the backlog and bypasses product-owner judgment.

distractors.text: Require stakeholders to submit only critical feedback and automatically discard the rest
distractors.trap: Filters too early; valuable insights may be lost.

distractors.text: Park all feedback until the release is complete, then schedule a dedicated cleanup sprint
distractors.trap: Defers learning and delays value from feedback.

distractors.text: Assign a full-time analyst to resolve every feedback item outside the team backlog
distractors.trap: Shadow work outside the backlog hides capacity and priority.

explanation: Value delivery performance domain: integrate feedback into normal prioritization so it competes on value with new work.

prompts: After the third increment of a delivery-robot project, field data shows the new path-finding algorithm raises battery use by 37%, dropping range below contractual limits. Increment four (high-res package scanning) is due in two weeks; stakeholders fear losing value if it slips, but range compliance is mandatory. Embracing incremental delivery, which course best preserves the product's benefits?

correct.text: Re-sequence the backlog to tackle battery optimization in the next increment, adjusting scope while keeping cadence.
correct.why: Steering the increment to the highest-risk, compliance-critical work preserves cadence and meets requirements.

distractors.text: Suspend upcoming increments, revert to the last stable code, and run a full architecture review first.
distractors.trap: Abandons cadence and the benefits of incremental delivery.

distractors.text: Tackle battery and scanning together via overtime to hold the schedule.
distractors.trap: Breaks sustainable pace and raises risk.

distractors.text: Proceed with scanning as planned and log the battery issue as future maintenance.
distractors.trap: Knowingly ships a non-compliant product.

explanation: Delivery/uncertainty performance domain: incremental delivery steers each cadence to the highest-value or highest-risk work.

prompts: In Scrum, what is the primary purpose of the sprint planning event?
What is the main reason a Scrum team holds sprint planning at the start of a sprint?
According to Scrum, what should sprint planning primarily accomplish?
Which outcome best describes the core purpose of the sprint planning ceremony?

correct.text: Define the sprint goal and select product backlog items for the sprint
correct.why: Sprint planning launches the sprint by setting the goal and forecasting the work.

correct.text: Establish a sprint goal and choose the backlog items the team will pursue
correct.why: The event sets why the sprint exists and what work will achieve that goal.

correct.text: Agree the sprint goal and forecast which product backlog items fit the sprint
correct.why: Goal plus selected work is the primary outcome of sprint planning.

distractors.text: Review completed work from the prior sprint and gather stakeholder feedback
distractors.trap: That is the sprint review, held at the sprint's end.

distractors.text: Identify and prioritize every risk that could arise during the sprint
distractors.trap: Risk handling is ongoing, not sprint planning's primary aim.

distractors.text: Produce a detailed plan for the whole project and assign every task
distractors.trap: Contradicts iterative, adaptive short-horizon planning.

distractors.text: Inspect team practices and commit to process improvements for the next cycle
distractors.trap: That is the retrospective, not sprint planning.

distractors.text: Demonstrate the increment and decide whether to release it to production
distractors.trap: Release and demo decisions belong to review or release planning.

distractors.text: Update the definition of done and re-baseline the project schedule
distractors.trap: Not the primary purpose of the sprint planning event.

explanation: Reflects the Delivery performance domain and empirical, iterative planning of adaptive approaches: sprint planning sets a goal and selects the work.

prompts: On an agile software project, market shifts have significantly changed customer needs. The product owner worries about the original deadline and budget. What should the team do?
Customer needs on your agile product have shifted after market changes, and the product owner frets about the original date and cost. What is the best response?
An agile team's customers now want different outcomes because the market moved. Leadership still cites the original deadline and budget. What should the team do?
Market conditions rewrote what customers value on your agile project. The product owner is anxious about fixed time and money. How should the team proceed?

correct.text: Collaborate with the customer to prioritize changes and deliver value incrementally
correct.why: Agile welcomes changing requirements and delivers highest-value work within constraints.

correct.text: Work with the customer to rank new needs and ship value in small increments
correct.why: Prioritized incremental delivery adapts to change without abandoning constraints.

correct.text: Partner with the customer on prioritization and keep delivering highest-value slices
correct.why: Value-first collaboration responds to change better than freezes or big-batch rewrites.

distractors.text: Work with the coach to freeze scope completely and block any further requirement changes
distractors.trap: Blocking change contradicts adaptability and value focus.

distractors.text: Renegotiate the contract for more time and budget before adapting any of the work
distractors.trap: Premature; adapt within constraints before expanding them.

distractors.text: Update the plan to incorporate every new requirement in one large replan batch
distractors.trap: Big-batch replanning abandons incremental delivery.

distractors.text: Ignore the market shift until the original committed scope is fully delivered as planned
distractors.trap: Delivers outdated value while needs have already moved.

distractors.text: Stop all delivery until a new detailed requirements document is signed off by everyone
distractors.trap: Heavy freeze stalls flow and fights agile change response.

distractors.text: Have developers choose which new needs to build based mainly on technical interest
distractors.trap: Technical preference is not customer value prioritization.

explanation: Tied to the 'embrace adaptability and change' principle and Delivery domain: prioritize with the customer and deliver value incrementally.

prompts: Your agile team flags critical technical debt that will slow future development, but management wants to keep adding features on schedule. What should you do?
Critical technical debt is blocking future pace, yet leaders insist on shipping features on the original plan. How should you respond?
The team surfaces high-impact technical debt that will hurt upcoming work, while management prioritizes feature throughput. What is your best action?
On an agile product, serious technical debt threatens sustainable delivery, but sponsors demand continuous feature output. What should you do?

correct.text: Advocate fixing the debt now, linking it to sustained delivery of working software
correct.why: Continuous technical excellence sustains agility and long-term value.

correct.text: Make the case to address the debt now so the team can keep shipping working software
correct.why: Paying down critical debt protects sustainable pace and product quality.

correct.text: Champion prompt attention to the debt as essential to ongoing delivery of working software
correct.why: Technical excellence is required to maintain agility over time.

distractors.text: Follow the plan, ship features first, and handle all of the debt in a later phase
distractors.trap: Waterfall-style deferral erodes sustainable pace.

distractors.text: Split the team so half clears debt while the other half continues building features
distractors.trap: Arbitrary split ignores value-based prioritization.

distractors.text: Document the debt thoroughly in a register and resolve it only after final release
distractors.trap: Favors documentation over working software.

distractors.text: Add overtime so the team can clear debt while still meeting every feature date
distractors.trap: Unsustainable pace and quality risk are not a fix.

distractors.text: Hide the debt from stakeholders until a quieter period after the product launches
distractors.trap: Lack of transparency lets risk grow unchallenged.

distractors.text: Rewrite the definition of done so accumulated debt no longer counts as incomplete work
distractors.trap: Gaming standards ignores the real delivery risk.

explanation: Delivery and Quality domains with the technical-excellence principle: address debt to keep delivering working software sustainably.

topic: Build quality in; do not deliver untested increment
prompts: Your team is approaching a major release, but a critical feature has not been fully tested and may not work as expected. What should you do?
A key feature is not fully tested as a major release nears, and behavior is uncertain. How should you proceed?
Release day is close, yet critical functionality still lacks thorough testing and may fail. What is the best action?
Stakeholders expect a major release soon, but one essential feature remains only partially tested. What should you do?

correct.text: Adjust the plan for full testing and communicate the revised schedule to stakeholders
correct.why: Quality is built in; shipping untested work sacrifices value and transparency.

correct.text: Replan so testing finishes properly, then share the updated schedule with stakeholders
correct.why: Transparent schedule change protects quality over calendar pressure.

correct.text: Make time to complete testing and inform stakeholders of the adjusted release date
correct.why: Built-in quality requires not releasing unfinished, untested functionality.

distractors.text: Release as planned and finish the remaining testing in the next iteration
distractors.trap: Ships potentially defective work to avoid delay.

distractors.text: Add resources to speed testing while keeping the original schedule fixed
distractors.trap: Assumes instant ramp-up; risks cost and quality.

distractors.text: Finish other features first and handle the critical untested piece afterward
distractors.trap: Risks delivering an incomplete, unusable product.

distractors.text: Ship with a known-issues list and promise a hotfix immediately after go-live
distractors.trap: Accepts known quality gaps to protect the date.

distractors.text: Skip remaining tests and rely on user acceptance feedback after the release
distractors.trap: Shifts defect discovery to customers.

distractors.text: Ask stakeholders to sign a waiver so the untested feature can still ship
distractors.trap: Waivers do not make defective value acceptable.

explanation: Quality performance domain: build quality in and stay transparent, making time to test rather than shipping unverified work.

topic: TDD: refactor, keep granularity, do not remove tests
prompts: A TDD unit-test suite now runs thirty minutes and threatens sprint goals. Developers suggest dropping tests or merging them into coarse integration checks. What best fits TDD principles?
Your TDD suite takes about half an hour, putting sprint goals at risk. Some want fewer tests or broad integration checks instead. What best aligns with TDD?
Unit tests under TDD have grown slow enough to endanger the sprint. The team proposes removing tests or consolidating into coarse checks. What should you favor?
A long-running TDD unit suite is blocking pace. Developers propose deleting tests or collapsing them into coarse integration suites. Which response fits TDD best?

correct.text: Profile and refactor the slow unit tests while preserving their granularity
correct.why: TDD needs fast, granular feedback; restore speed without losing precision.

correct.text: Find and fix the slow tests so they stay fine-grained and run quickly again
correct.why: Speed and diagnostic detail both matter; refactor rather than thin coverage.

correct.text: Optimize the slow unit tests through profiling while keeping tests focused and granular
correct.why: TDD feedback loops depend on fast, pinpoint unit tests.

distractors.text: Stub database calls and drop assertions, leaving lightweight checks for major failures
distractors.trap: Dropping assertions discards the safety net.

distractors.text: Replace granular unit tests with broad integration checks so fewer run
distractors.trap: Loses pinpoint feedback that traces defects.

distractors.text: Defer optimization to release stabilization and add more test cases meanwhile
distractors.trap: Leaves the build slow and confidence eroding.

distractors.text: Delete the slowest half of the suite to meet sprint goals this week
distractors.trap: Sacrifices coverage for short-term schedule relief.

distractors.text: Move all unit tests to a weekly batch job so daily work is not blocked
distractors.trap: Delayed feedback breaks the TDD cycle.

distractors.text: Keep the suite as-is and accept that sprint goals will slip each cycle
distractors.trap: Ignores the feedback-speed problem TDD requires fixing.

explanation: Quality performance domain: TDD's fast, granular feedback loop is preserved by refactoring, not by deleting tests.

topic: Adaptive planning; reprioritize on user feedback
prompts: After three sprints, beta results show most clinicians find the data-entry workflow cumbersome. The product owner wants a redesign that would postpone two features and add about two sprints, while leadership pushes the original date. Applying adaptive planning, what should you do?

Beta users report a painful data-entry flow after three sprints. The product owner proposes a redesign costing roughly two sprints and two deferred features; leadership still wants the original date. What does adaptive planning call for?

Clinician feedback after three sprints flags a cumbersome workflow. A redesign would slip two features and about two sprints, yet leaders insist on the original timeline. How should you apply adaptive planning?

Empirical beta feedback shows the main workflow is hard to use. Redesign would delay two features about two sprints; sponsors still push the original release. What should you do under adaptive planning?

correct.text: Facilitate stakeholder trade-offs, then jointly adjust scope or timeline based on value
correct.why: Adaptive planning uses empirical feedback through value-based, collaborative decisions.

correct.text: Lead stakeholders through trade-offs and jointly revise scope or schedule by value
correct.why: Collaboration on value trade-offs is how adaptive plans respond to learning.

correct.text: Convene stakeholders to weigh options, then agree value-driven scope or date changes
correct.why: Empirical feedback guides joint scope-timeline choices, not rigid plan-following.

distractors.text: Add people and extend hours to deliver the redesign and still hit the original date
distractors.trap: Violates sustainable pace; late-added staff slow projects.

distractors.text: Do a risk assessment and then let senior leadership make the final call alone
distractors.trap: Abdicates the PM's facilitation of collaborative decisions.

distractors.text: Finish the current backlog as planned and defer the redesign to a later release
distractors.trap: Rigid plan-following ignores critical user feedback.

distractors.text: Cancel the beta program so negative feedback cannot delay the planned release
distractors.trap: Hides learning instead of adapting to it.

distractors.text: Approve the redesign unilaterally without involving leadership or key users
distractors.trap: Skips collaborative trade-off decisions stakeholders own.

distractors.text: Keep the date and quietly drop quality checks to free capacity for both tracks
distractors.trap: Sacrifices quality and transparency for calendar optics.

explanation: Delivery domain and adaptive planning: respond to feedback with value-based, collaborative scope and timeline decisions.

topic: PO reorders sprint backlog for critical bug
prompts: Mid-sprint a critical payment bug hits production, threatening customer trust, while marketing still wants an upsell feature promised in next week's demo. Capacity allows only one focus change. As product owner, what best maximizes value?
A production payment defect is harming trust mid-sprint, and marketing expects an upsell feature in next week's demo. You can change only one focus. As product owner, what maximizes value?
During the sprint, a severe live payment bug emerges while marketing pushes an upsell item for the upcoming demo. Only one priority shift is feasible. What should the product owner do?
Customer trust is at risk from a critical payment bug found mid-sprint; marketing still wants the demoed upsell feature. Capacity supports one change. As PO, what is best?

correct.text: Re-order the sprint backlog with the team to replace the upsell feature with the bug fix
correct.why: Fixing a live, high-impact defect delivers more immediate value than a new feature.

correct.text: Work with the team to swap the upsell item for the critical payment bug fix
correct.why: Protecting customers from a live defect outranks a promotional feature.

correct.text: Reprioritize the sprint so the bug fix takes the place of the upsell feature
correct.why: PO-led reordering puts highest current value—stability—ahead of new work.

distractors.text: Wait until the sprint review, gathering more stakeholder data first
distractors.trap: Leaves the defect live, harming customers and value.

distractors.text: Escalate to marketing and ask them to authorize delaying both items
distractors.trap: Abdicates the PO's authority to set priority.

distractors.text: Keep the plan and have developers work overtime to deliver both
distractors.trap: Breaks sustainable pace and risks quality.

distractors.text: Ship a cosmetic patch for the demo while deferring the real payment fix
distractors.trap: Masks risk; customers still face the critical defect.

distractors.text: Freeze the sprint backlog because mid-sprint changes are never allowed
distractors.trap: Ignores PO right to maximize value when reality shifts.

distractors.text: Assign the bug to a support team outside the sprint without changing priorities
distractors.trap: Hides capacity use and may delay a critical fix.

explanation: Delivery domain: the product owner maximizes value by renegotiating the sprint backlog toward the highest-value work.

topic: Root cause plus stakeholder collaboration on defect
prompts: In a retail inventory project using incremental delivery, stakeholders note that a prior increment's count accuracy dropped due to timing conflicts between mobile updates and the central database, while a barcode-scanning increment is set to launch with a CEO press release. How should you balance these?

On an incremental warehouse system, count accuracy fell after mobile-to-database timing conflicts, yet a barcode-scanning release is tied to an executive press event. How do you balance quality and commitment?
Stakeholders report accuracy issues from sync timing in a prior retail inventory increment, while the next barcode feature is locked to a CEO announcement. What is the best way to balance both?
A prior increment's inventory counts degraded from mobile and database timing conflicts; the upcoming barcode increment has a high-visibility launch. How should you proceed?

correct.text: Root-cause the accuracy issue, then decide with stakeholders: next increment or parallel debt work
correct.why: Investigate first, then decide collaboratively, balancing commitment and quality.

correct.text: Diagnose the accuracy drop, then choose with stakeholders a next-increment fix or parallel debt work
correct.why: Root-cause understanding plus stakeholder choice balances quality and commitment.

correct.text: Assess root cause of the accuracy drop and collaboratively plan fix timing with stakeholders
correct.why: Evidence-based, joint planning avoids both blind delay and blind deferral.

distractors.text: Delay the next increment a sprint to fix accuracy first and reschedule the press release
distractors.trap: Reacts before understanding the fix's scope.

distractors.text: Proceed as planned and defer all mobile-database sync issues to a later increment
distractors.trap: Accepts a known defect on an unstable foundation.

distractors.text: Split the team so one group fixes accuracy and one builds barcode, then merge later
distractors.trap: Unanalyzed split risks integration defects and lower velocity.

distractors.text: Cancel the press release immediately and halt all barcode development until accuracy is perfect
distractors.trap: Overreacts without assessing impact or options.

distractors.text: Blame the prior team and demand a full rewrite of the accuracy module before anything else
distractors.trap: Blame and rewrite skip root-cause analysis and collaboration.

distractors.text: Ship barcode as planned and mark the accuracy problem as an accepted residual risk only
distractors.trap: May ignore a fixable quality issue without joint review.

explanation: Delivery and Quality domains with stakeholder engagement: diagnose root cause, then decide collaboratively how to sequence the fix.

topic: Incorporate integration fixes into current increment
prompts: During incremental delivery of a complex system, the team finds that a feature from the previous increment is causing conflicts with new work. What is the best action?
While building the next increment, the team discovers a prior feature conflicts with current work. What should they do?
Integration issues appear when new work collides with a feature delivered last increment. What is the best response?
On a multi-increment system, a previously delivered feature now conflicts with ongoing development. How should the team act?

correct.text: Assess impact, prioritize the fixes, and fold needed changes into the current increment
correct.why: Prompt integration fixes protect system integrity and keep work flowing.

correct.text: Evaluate impact, rank needed fixes, and include them in the current increment work
correct.why: Addressing conflicts now preserves integrity better than deferral or restarts.

correct.text: Analyze the conflicts, prioritize remediation, and integrate fixes into this increment
correct.why: Current-increment remediation maintains flow and system coherence.

distractors.text: Revert to the state before the conflicting feature and restart development from there
distractors.trap: Extreme; discards valid progress.

distractors.text: Halt all development work until every integration conflict is fully resolved end-to-end
distractors.trap: Overreaction that stalls the whole project.

distractors.text: Continue planned work and handle conflicts only in a future dedicated debugging increment
distractors.trap: Defers, compounding issues and technical debt.

distractors.text: Ignore the conflicts and ship both features with a known-issues note for users
distractors.trap: Leaves integration defects in production value.

distractors.text: Create a separate team to rebuild the entire previous increment in parallel from scratch
distractors.trap: Costly parallel rebuild without impact-based prioritization.

distractors.text: Escalate to the sponsor and freeze the product until a new architecture is fully approved
distractors.trap: Escalation and freeze overreact before local remediation.

explanation: Delivery domain: integrate fixes into current work to keep the increment stable and maintain flow.

prompts: After many stakeholder sessions, your backlog has grown large, with several important features spread across overlapping items causing confusion. How can you best consolidate it for clear, value-focused planning?
Your product backlog is bloated; related needs sit in overlapping items that confuse planning. What best consolidates it for value-focused work?
Stakeholder workshops left a large, messy backlog with important features duplicated across items. How do you best restore clarity for planning?
Overlapping backlog items from many sessions make prioritization hard. What approach best consolidates work around clear value themes?

correct.text: Hold refinement with the team and stakeholders to group related items into thematic epics
correct.why: Collaborative refinement consolidates the backlog while preserving real user needs.

correct.text: Facilitate collaborative refinement that clusters overlapping items into epics by common themes
correct.why: Joint refinement clarifies structure without discarding user-specific value.

correct.text: Run a refinement session to organize related backlog items into epics around shared themes
correct.why: Thematic epics restore planning clarity through shared understanding.

distractors.text: Have assigned members rewrite all items into broader requirements purely for speed
distractors.trap: Sacrifices user-specific detail for expediency.

distractors.text: Build the easiest items first and postpone any consolidation until much later
distractors.trap: Neglects important features; defers the real problem.

distractors.text: Proceed to implementation and refine the overlapping items during active development
distractors.trap: Leaves duplication and confusion unresolved.

distractors.text: Delete half the backlog items arbitrarily to force a smaller, simpler list of work
distractors.trap: Arbitrary cuts risk losing important stakeholder needs.

distractors.text: Convert every item into a fixed work package owned by a single department silo
distractors.trap: Silo ownership fragments value rather than clarifying themes.

distractors.text: Stop stakeholder input until the current backlog is fully delivered as written
distractors.trap: Blocks refinement and freezes a confused structure.

explanation: Delivery and Scope domains: collaborative backlog refinement organizes work into value-focused epics.

prompts: On day three of a two-week sprint, the team finds one committed item needs three extra days because a third-party API changed its authentication. The product owner worries about the sprint goal of end-to-end shipment tracking. What should the team do?
Midway into a two-week sprint, an item needs three more days after a third-party API auth change. The sprint goal is end-to-end shipment tracking. How should the team respond?
Day three of a sprint reveals that a committed story needs three extra days due to changed API authentication. The PO fears for the shipment-tracking goal. What is best?
A third-party API authentication change inflates one sprint item by three days. The sprint goal is end-to-end shipment tracking. What should the team do?

correct.text: Collaborate with the product owner to drop lower-priority items if needed to protect the goal
correct.why: The sprint goal is the commitment; scope may be renegotiated as learning emerges.

correct.text: Work with the product owner to re-scope so core tracking can still meet the sprint goal
correct.why: Protect the goal by renegotiating item scope inside the fixed timebox.

correct.text: Partner with the product owner to keep core tracking and renegotiate lower-priority commitments
correct.why: Sprint success is goal achievement, not delivering every selected item.

distractors.text: Log the API change as a blocker, finish remaining items, and defer the integration work next sprint
distractors.trap: Abandons the core commitment and risks the sprint goal.

distractors.text: Keep all committed items in the sprint and extend the sprint by three days for the auth fix
distractors.trap: Sprint length is fixed; extending violates the timebox.

distractors.text: Escalate to the Scrum Master and bring in temporary developers to absorb the extra three days
distractors.trap: Mid-sprint staffing additions disrupt flow and slow delivery.

distractors.text: Cancel the sprint immediately and restart planning from scratch with an entirely new goal
distractors.trap: Premature cancellation when the goal may still be reachable.

distractors.text: Ignore the estimate increase and pressure the team to finish everything on the original date
distractors.trap: Denies reality and invites quality and burnout issues.

distractors.text: Remove the sprint goal entirely so any completed subset of items can count as success
distractors.trap: Erases the shared commitment that guides trade-offs.

explanation: Delivery and Scope domains: protect the sprint goal by renegotiating scope with the product owner, not extending the sprint.

prompts: In a five-day sprint, a key developer becomes unavailable on day two, putting three high-priority items behind, though the sprint goal is still reachable if work is reallocated. As the coach, what best preserves the time-box and the goal?
Day two of a five-day sprint, a critical developer is out; three high-priority items lag, but the goal remains possible if work is redistributed. As coach, what best protects the timebox and goal?
A key person drops out mid-sprint on day two of five. High-priority items slip, yet reallocation could still hit the goal. What should the coach facilitate?
Lost capacity mid-sprint threatens three high-priority items, but the sprint goal can still be met with reallocation. As coach, what is the best move?

correct.text: Facilitate reprioritization and redistribute affected items among remaining members
correct.why: Self-organizing inside the fixed timebox keeps focus on the sprint goal.

correct.text: Help the team re-prioritize and reassign impacted work across those still available
correct.why: Redistribution by the team preserves the goal without breaking the timebox.

correct.text: Coach the team to rebalance and share the affected items among remaining members
correct.why: Self-organization solves capacity loss better than extending or hiding work.

distractors.text: Extend the sprint by two days and keep every original assignment unchanged
distractors.trap: Breaks the fixed sprint time-box.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to swap the stories for easier items from the backlog
distractors.trap: Shifts scope rather than solving capacity.

distractors.text: Hand the unfinished work to a parallel task force operating outside the sprint
distractors.trap: Adds hidden work; undermines transparency.

distractors.text: Cancel the sprint goal entirely and treat any completed output as acceptable
distractors.trap: Abandons the focus that guides self-organization.

distractors.text: Order remaining developers to work overtime until the original plan is fully met
distractors.trap: Unsustainable pace; coach should enable self-organization.

distractors.text: Pause the sprint until the key developer returns the following week
distractors.trap: Breaks the timebox and wastes remaining capacity.

explanation: Team performance domain: self-organizing teams reallocate work within the fixed time-box to protect the sprint goal.

prompts: Six months in, data shows mobile shopping surged while your outputs focus on desktop web; the project is on time and budget, and some team members argue that pivoting would waste completed work. What best ensures optimal value?
Analytics show mobile commerce has overtaken desktop, but your project still delivers desktop-first. It is on schedule and budget; some say a pivot wastes sunk work. What maximizes value?
Customer data now favors mobile shopping over desktop, yet delivery remains desktop-centric and on plan. Team members fear rework waste. What best optimizes value?
Mid-project, evidence points to mobile demand while outputs stay desktop-focused. Schedule and budget are healthy; some resist pivoting. How do you ensure optimal value?

correct.text: Assess mobile-pivot value versus rework cost, then present options to stakeholders for a decision
correct.why: Weigh future value, not sunk cost, and let stakeholders decide with data.

correct.text: Weigh a mobile pivot against rework impact, then bring scope options to stakeholders
correct.why: Data-backed options beat assuming either stay-the-course or dual delivery.

correct.text: Compare pivot value with rework impact and facilitate a stakeholder scope decision
correct.why: Sunk cost is irrelevant; future value and governance drive the call.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor for more funding and time to complete both desktop and mobile tracks
distractors.trap: Assumes a solution before analysis.

distractors.text: Continue desktop as planned solely because the original business case was approved
distractors.trap: Process adherence over shifting customer value.

distractors.text: Add mobile as supplementary work while keeping desktop as the primary focus by default
distractors.trap: Presumes the answer before the analysis.

distractors.text: Discard all desktop work immediately and restart the effort as a pure mobile project
distractors.trap: Extreme pivot without assessing value and rework.

distractors.text: Ignore the mobile demand data because the team already invested six months of effort
distractors.trap: Sunk-cost fallacy; past spend is not future value.

distractors.text: Let developers choose the platform based on their preferred technology stack alone
distractors.trap: Technical preference is not a value-based scope decision.

explanation: Delivery domain and value focus: assess future value over sunk cost, then decide scope with stakeholders.

prompts: On a healthcare software project, your senior systems analyst is doing work junior members could handle, while complex architectural decisions stall because only she has the expertise. The schedule is slipping and juniors feel underutilized. What is the most suitable resource management action?
A senior analyst is buried in junior-level tasks while architecture waits on her unique skill. Schedule slips and juniors feel idle. What resource action fits best?
Complex design is blocked because the only expert is doing routine work juniors could own. Delivery is late and juniors are underused. What should you do?
Your expert systems analyst handles simple work while specialist decisions queue up. Juniors are underutilized and the plan is slipping. What is the best resource fix?

correct.text: Reassign work to match skills: juniors take routine tasks; the analyst owns specialist decisions
correct.why: Skill-to-task matching unblocks the schedule and uses people appropriately.

correct.text: Shift routine work to juniors and free the senior analyst for complex architecture decisions
correct.why: Immediate realignment restores capacity where expertise is scarce.

correct.text: Align tasks to capability so juniors handle routine work and the analyst focuses on architecture
correct.why: Matching skills to tasks resolves misallocation and stalled decisions now.

distractors.text: Launch a mentoring program where the analyst trains juniors on complex tasks for the project duration
distractors.trap: Builds future capability but leaves decisions stalled now.

distractors.text: Run a skills-assessment workshop and build a full competency matrix for future assignments only
distractors.trap: Valuable long-term, but ignores the immediate schedule impact.

distractors.text: Hold career-development meetings with juniors and adjust roles mainly to their growth goals
distractors.trap: Focuses on long-term growth, not current allocation.

distractors.text: Add another senior hire at full cost and leave every current assignment exactly as it is
distractors.trap: Costly; does not fix misuse of the expert already on the team.

distractors.text: Have the analyst work overtime covering both the routine tasks and the complex architecture work
distractors.trap: Unsustainable and still underuses junior capacity.

distractors.text: Outsource all architectural decisions externally so the analyst can remain on routine tasks
distractors.trap: Wastes internal expertise and may create knowledge gaps.

explanation: Reflects the Team performance domain: effective resource allocation matches competencies to task needs to sustain flow.

topic: Strategic alignment via gap analysis and scope adjustment
prompts: Your board authorized a five-year plan to lead in rural telemedicine. Your patient-portal project's requirements, however, emphasize urban clinic scheduling and large-hospital integration, and the sponsor has already funded that scope. How do you best ensure the project supports the strategic direction?
Strategy targets rural telemedicine leadership, but your funded portal scope centers on urban clinics and large hospitals. How do you best align the project to strategy?

The board's five-year direction is rural telemedicine, yet the patient portal still prioritizes urban scheduling and hospital integration with sponsor funding locked. What best restores strategic fit?
Your project scope serves urban clinics while organizational strategy aims at rural telemedicine. The sponsor funded the current scope. How do you ensure strategic support?

correct.text: Run a gap analysis against strategy, then propose sponsor scope changes for rural telemedicine
correct.why: Evidence-based scope proposals respect governance while closing the strategy gap.

correct.text: Compare scope to strategic goals, then recommend sponsor-approved rural telemedicine changes
correct.why: Gap analysis plus formal scope change keeps strategy and governance aligned.

correct.text: Analyze the strategy gap and present sponsor scope adjustments adding rural telemedicine
correct.why: Projects follow strategy; propose changes with evidence rather than ignore the gap.

distractors.text: Proceed with current requirements to protect funding, and propose rural features later as a follow-up
distractors.trap: Builds misaligned output and delays strategic value.

distractors.text: Complete a full urban-versus-rural market study and wait for leadership before any more work
distractors.trap: Unnecessary delay; the strategy is already set by the board.

distractors.text: Request a workshop to revise the five-year plan so it fits the existing project scope
distractors.trap: Reverses hierarchy; strategy should not bend to one project.

distractors.text: Silently add rural features without changing the approved scope baseline or sponsor approval
distractors.trap: Bypasses governance and sponsor authorization.

distractors.text: Cancel the project immediately because the current scope is not fully rural-telemedicine focused
distractors.trap: Extreme; gap analysis and scope change may realign it.

distractors.text: Keep urban scope and rebrand deliverables as rural telemedicine in stakeholder communications only
distractors.trap: Cosmetic labeling without real strategic capability.

explanation: Reflects the Stewardship principle and Delivery domain: projects exist to convert organizational strategy into value.

prompts: A video-streaming start-up has pivoted from one-time film sales to recurring subscriptions as its main revenue driver. Your app-upgrade project still centers on in-app purchases and omits subscriber engagement features. Investors ask for a status update midway through. What best maintains alignment with the pivot?
