distractors.text: Negotiate a one-week timebox extension with the product owner so the team can address the architectural flaw and still deliver all five features.
distractors.trap: Extending the timebox breaks timeboxing discipline.

distractors.text: Ask QA to reduce their testing scope on the three affected features so the development team gains time to implement the proper fix.
distractors.trap: Cutting test coverage sacrifices quality in the timebox.

distractors.text: Cancel the review and keep coding until all five features meet the original commitment.
distractors.trap: Ignores the timebox end and stakeholder cadence.

distractors.text: Drop all five features from the review and present only the architectural redesign plan.
distractors.trap: Fails to deliver incremental value within the timebox.

distractors.text: Force overtime so the team completes a full proper fix for every affected feature this timebox.
distractors.trap: Unsustainable pressure and may still miss the limit.

explanation: Delivery performance domain and Quality: with time fixed, flex scope while building quality in.

prompts: On a data center migration, Activity K has Early Start day 25, Early Finish day 33, Late Start day 29, and Late Finish day 37. Your team lead asks whether K can be delayed to accommodate a training session on days 26-28. What should you advise?

correct.text: Activity K can be delayed up to its 4 days of total float before affecting the critical path.
correct.why: Total float equals Late Start minus Early Start (29 - 25 = 4), so the delay fits.

distractors.text: It can shift 3 days only if successors are also moved by adjusting dependencies.
distractors.trap: 3 days fits within float; no successor changes needed

distractors.text: It should start on day 29 since the Late Start is the optimal start point.
distractors.trap: Late Start consumes all float, not 'optimal'

distractors.text: It can be delayed up to 8 days, from Late Finish minus Early Start.
distractors.trap: Wrong formula; that is not float

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: total float is the delay allowed without impacting the end date.

prompts: Implementing a warehouse management system, the scheduler finds Activity T (testing) has Early Start 35, Early Finish 48, Late Start 40, Late Finish 53. Activity U (user training) follows T with an Early Start of 52. How much Free Float does Activity T have before it delays U's start?

correct.text: 4 days: successor's Early Start (52) minus Activity T's Early Finish (48).
correct.why: Free float is the delay allowed without pushing the successor's early start.

distractors.text: 5 days: Late Start (40) minus Early Start (35).
distractors.trap: That is total float, not free float

distractors.text: 13 days: Late Finish (53) minus Late Start (40) plus a buffer.
distractors.trap: Not a valid free-float formula

distractors.text: 8 days: total float added to a Late Finish/successor difference.
distractors.trap: Invented formula with no basis

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: free float is successor Early Start minus current Early Finish.

prompts: Reviewing your Gantt chart, you find Task B is scheduled to finish before its predecessor, Task A, because of an incorrect dependency setting. What should you do to correct it?
On the project Gantt, Task B is set to complete before predecessor Task A due to a bad dependency. How should you fix the schedule logic?
A schedule review shows Task B finishing ahead of its predecessor Task A because the dependency was defined incorrectly. What is the right correction?
Your Gantt has Task B ending before Task A, even though A is the predecessor, after a dependency misconfiguration. What should you do?

correct.text: Fix the dependency so Task A finishes before Task B starts.
correct.why: Correcting the logical relationship lets the schedule recalculate.

correct.text: Correct the link so Task A completes before Task B begins.
correct.why: Restoring proper predecessor logic drives valid dates.

correct.text: Update the dependency so predecessor Task A ends before successor Task B starts.
correct.why: Right dependency definition yields a coherent schedule.

distractors.text: Manually change the start and finish dates of both tasks.
distractors.trap: Treats the symptom, not the wrong logic.

distractors.text: Add resources to Task B so it finishes before Task A starts.
distractors.trap: Ignores the required sequence of work.

distractors.text: Let Task B start before Task A to speed completion.
distractors.trap: Disregards the logical dependency and adds risk.

distractors.text: Delete both tasks and re-enter them without links.
distractors.trap: Loses structure instead of fixing the relationship.

distractors.text: Mark Task B as complete early to clear the conflict visually.
distractors.trap: Falsifies progress rather than fixing logic.

distractors.text: Add lag so both tasks can finish on the same day.
distractors.trap: Does not restore the required predecessor order.

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: model correct logical dependencies rather than manipulating dates.

prompts: During planning for a two-week iteration, the team set a four-hour timebox. At three hours fifty minutes, two complex backlog items remain unestimated. The product owner says the estimates are crucial for tomorrow's forecast and wants to keep going. Committed to timeboxing, what should you do?

Sprint planning is timeboxed to four hours for a two-week iteration. With ten minutes left, two hard backlog items still lack estimates. The product owner urges continuing for tomorrow's forecast. What should you do to honor the timebox?
A two-week iteration's planning session has a four-hour limit. Near the end, two complex items are still unestimated; the product owner wants to extend for the forecast. How do you uphold timeboxing?
Four hours were set for planning a two-week sprint. At three hours fifty minutes, two tough items remain unsized and the product owner pushes to continue. What should you do?

correct.text: Stop at the four-hour limit and schedule a separate refinement slot to size the remaining items.
correct.why: The timebox is fixed; remaining sizing moves to later.

correct.text: End when four hours are up and book a follow-up refinement to estimate the leftover items.
correct.why: Timebox ends on schedule; unfinished estimates get a new slot.

correct.text: Close the session at the timebox limit and plan another refinement to size what remains.
correct.why: Protecting the timebox preserves cadence; work continues later.

distractors.text: Extend the meeting until every item is estimated for tomorrow's planning.
distractors.trap: Extending violates the fixed timebox.

distractors.text: Take a vote and, if most agree, keep going and adjust later.
distractors.trap: Group consent does not override the timebox.

distractors.text: Split the group so a subset stays to finish estimating.
distractors.trap: Breaks collective ownership and the timebox.

distractors.text: Assign rough placeholder sizes so the forecast can proceed tonight.
distractors.trap: False precision undermines forecast quality.

distractors.text: Drop the two items from the backlog until the next release planning.
distractors.trap: Avoids sizing instead of refining separately.

distractors.text: Let the product owner estimate alone after the team leaves.
distractors.trap: Bypasses team-owned estimates and collaboration.

explanation: Delivery performance domain: timeboxes are fixed constraints that protect the empirical control.

prompts: In a project schedule, the critical path is best defined as the ____.
Which statement best defines the critical path in a project schedule?
When describing schedule network analysis, the critical path is best described as the ____.
For schedule control purposes, how is the critical path best defined?

correct.text: Sequence of tasks that determines the project's overall duration.
correct.why: The critical path has no float; delays there delay the project.

correct.text: Longest path of dependent activities that sets total project duration.
correct.why: Zero-float path governs when the project can finish.

correct.text: Chain of activities whose total duration defines the project end date.
correct.why: Any slip on this path extends overall project duration.

distractors.text: Chain of the most time-consuming tasks.
distractors.trap: Long tasks may have float and not be critical.

distractors.text: List of the highest-priority tasks.
distractors.trap: Priority is unrelated to zero float.

distractors.text: Set of tasks that must be completed first.
distractors.trap: Early tasks need not determine total duration.

distractors.text: Collection of all tasks assigned to critical resources.
distractors.trap: Resource criticality is not the schedule critical path.

distractors.text: Activities with the largest remaining effort estimates.
distractors.trap: Effort size alone does not define the path.

distractors.text: Work packages that carry the highest risk ratings.
distractors.trap: Risk rating is separate from path float analysis.

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: the critical path is the longest zero-float path setting duration.

topic: Update dependency to reflect actual work
prompts: Reviewing your Gantt chart, you see 'System Configuration' (Task K) has a finish-to-finish dependency with 'Data Migration' (Task L), but K's bar extends three days past L's finish. The team lead confirms configuration genuinely needs those extra days. What is the most appropriate action?
On the Gantt, System Configuration (K) is finish-to-finish with Data Migration (L), yet K runs three days beyond L. The lead confirms those days are truly required. What should you do?
Task K (System Configuration) is linked finish-to-finish to Task L (Data Migration), but K still needs three days after L ends. The team lead affirms that need is real. What is the best action?

Your schedule shows finish-to-finish between System Configuration and Data Migration, with configuration lasting three days past migration. The lead says that extra work is genuine. How should you respond?

correct.text: Review and update the dependency type to reflect the real relationship, then assess downstream impacts.
correct.why: The schedule should model actual work logic, not force wrong links.

correct.text: Adjust the dependency to match how the work truly relates, then evaluate schedule impacts.
correct.why: Logic must mirror reality; then check effects on successors.

correct.text: Correct the link so it represents the real dependency and review effects downstream.
correct.why: Accurate relationships keep forecasts valid after the change.

distractors.text: Add resources to Task K to compress it three days and finish together.
distractors.trap: Crashes work the lead says is genuinely needed.

distractors.text: Convert to start-to-start and adjust both durations proportionally.
distractors.trap: Arbitrary change not based on work logic.

distractors.text: Extend Task L by three days to keep the finish-to-finish tie.
distractors.trap: Pads a task to satisfy a wrong dependency.

distractors.text: Hide Task K's overrun with a manual date lock on both bars.
distractors.trap: Masks the problem without correcting logic.

distractors.text: Remove Task K from the critical reports until migration ends.
distractors.trap: Hides reality instead of modeling true work.

distractors.text: Leave the chart as-is and note the mismatch only in meeting minutes.
distractors.trap: Informal notes do not fix the schedule model.

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: the schedule must reflect reality through correct dependency logic.

prompts: Building a schedule for a mobile app launch: end-user validation testing needs the backend API partially complete, frontend development can start once UI/UX designs are final, and app store submission needs both legal's privacy-policy review and marketing's app description. The sponsor asks which activities can run in parallel. Which statement is accurate?

For a mobile app release schedule, validation testing waits on a partially ready backend API, frontend can begin when UI/UX designs are final, and store submission needs legal privacy review plus marketing's description. Which activities can truly run in parallel?

A consumer app launch plan shows: user validation depends on a partial backend API; frontend starts after UI/UX is final; store submission needs legal and marketing inputs. Which statement about parallel work is correct?

Scheduling a mobile launch: validation testing needs backend API partially done; frontend starts once UI/UX is finalized; store submission needs legal privacy review and marketing copy. Which parallel-work statement is accurate?

correct.text: Backend API and frontend development can overlap, since they have different predecessors and can proceed after their own triggers.
correct.why: Independent predecessors let the two activities run concurrently.

correct.text: Backend API work and frontend development may run together because each starts from its own independent predecessor.
correct.why: Separate triggers allow concurrent progress and a shorter timeline.

correct.text: Frontend development and backend API work can proceed in parallel once each meets its own start condition.
correct.why: Different predecessors enable overlap without false sequencing.

distractors.text: User validation testing and app store submission can run in parallel as both depend on upstream deliverables.
distractors.trap: They sit at different points and do not overlap.

distractors.text: Frontend must wait for backend completion because UI/UX depends on the architecture.
distractors.trap: Frontend only needs finalized UI/UX designs.

distractors.text: Legal review and marketing should be sequenced consecutively to coordinate before submission.
distractors.trap: They are independent and can run in parallel.

distractors.text: All launch activities must be fully sequential to protect quality gates.
distractors.trap: Over-serializes work that can safely overlap.

distractors.text: App store submission can start as soon as frontend development begins.
distractors.trap: Submission needs legal and marketing, not just frontend start.

distractors.text: Validation testing can begin immediately with no backend progress required.
distractors.trap: Validation needs the backend API at least partially complete.

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: activities with independent predecessors can run concurrently.

prompts: On an infrastructure upgrade, schedule analysis gives Activity F an Early Start of day 20, Early Finish day 28, Late Start day 24, and Late Finish day 32. A subcontractor asks to start F on day 22 instead of 20 to coordinate with another project. How should you respond?

correct.text: The request is acceptable: F has 4 days of total float, so a day-22 start stays within the Late Start of day 24.
correct.why: Total float (24 - 20 = 4) allows a two-day delay without affecting completion.

distractors.text: Acceptable because F has 8 days of free float allowing a 2-day shift.
distractors.trap: 8 days of float is miscalculated

distractors.text: Decline it, since any deviation from Early Start delays the project.
distractors.trap: Positive float absorbs delay without impact

distractors.text: It needs Monte Carlo simulation before deciding.
distractors.trap: Overkill for a simple float calculation

explanation: Schedule/Delivery performance domain: total float permits a delayed start up to the Late Start.

prompts: You are reviewing the network diagram for a product development effort. The testing phase has a finish-to-finish relationship with the documentation phase, and the documentation team says it needs the final test results before it can wrap up. What does this relationship tell you about how the two activities are scheduled?
In a product network diagram, testing and documentation share a finish-to-finish link; documentation needs final test results to close. What does that relationship mean for scheduling?
Testing is finish-to-finish with documentation on your product schedule, and docs cannot finish without final test results. How should you interpret that relationship?
A network review shows documentation finish-to-finish with testing because final results are needed to complete the docs. What does this relationship indicate?

correct.text: Documentation cannot finish until testing finishes; the two may run in parallel but their completions are linked.
correct.why: Finish-to-finish ties successor finish to predecessor finish, not starts.

correct.text: Docs may proceed alongside testing, but documentation cannot complete until testing is complete.
correct.why: FF links finishes so both can overlap until linked ends.

correct.text: The phases can overlap, yet documentation's finish waits on testing's finish.
correct.why: A finish-to-finish constraint couples completion dates only.

distractors.text: Testing must be fully complete before documentation can begin.
distractors.trap: Describes finish-to-start, not finish-to-finish.

distractors.text: The two must start together because they share resources.
distractors.trap: FF links finishes, not starts or resources.

distractors.text: Documentation's finish date controls when testing must finish.
distractors.trap: Reverses the dependency direction.

distractors.text: Neither activity may start until both teams are fully staffed.
distractors.trap: Staffing is unrelated to finish-to-finish logic.

distractors.text: Testing duration must equal documentation duration exactly.
distractors.trap: FF does not force equal activity lengths.

distractors.text: Documentation automatically inherits testing's start date.
distractors.trap: FF does not dictate successor start timing.

explanation: Tests sequencing logic within the Planning performance domain: a finish-to-finish dependency links activity completions, not their start dates.

prompts: Midway through a project you apply fast tracking, and your next status report shows a positive schedule variance. What does this indicate?
After mid-project fast tracking, the status report reports a positive schedule variance. What does that mean?
You used fast tracking partway through delivery, and earned-value results now show positive schedule variance. What does this indicate?
Halfway into execution, following a fast-track decision, schedule variance is positive on the status report. How should you interpret it?

correct.text: The project is ahead of schedule.
correct.why: Positive SV (EV − PV) means earned work exceeds planned work.

correct.text: Work is progressing faster than the baseline plan.
correct.why: Positive schedule variance signals performance ahead of plan.

correct.text: Schedule performance is better than planned to date.
correct.why: SV > 0 indicates the project is ahead of schedule.

distractors.text: The project is behind schedule.
distractors.trap: A negative SV, not positive, signals a slip.

distractors.text: The project is over budget.
distractors.trap: Budget status is shown by cost variance.

distractors.text: The project is exactly on schedule.
distractors.trap: On-schedule is SV = 0, not positive.

distractors.text: The project has exhausted its contingency reserve.
distractors.trap: Reserves are not measured by schedule variance.

distractors.text: The critical path has automatically lengthened.
distractors.trap: Positive SV does not imply a longer path.

distractors.text: Scope has increased without a change request.
distractors.trap: SV measures schedule, not unauthorized scope growth.

explanation: Measurement performance domain: schedule variance is an earned value metric where a positive value means ahead of plan.

prompts: Your Gantt chart defines a start-to-start relationship between Task G (predecessor) and Task H (successor), but Task H is currently scheduled to start before Task G. Absent any resource or other constraints, what is the best course of action?
A start-to-start link ties predecessor Task G to successor Task H, yet H is set to start before G. With no resource constraints, what should you do?
On the Gantt, Tasks G and H are start-to-start (G predecessor), but H begins earlier than G. No other constraints apply. What is the best fix?
Schedule logic shows start-to-start from Task G to Task H, while H is planned to start first. Ignoring resource limits, how should you correct this?

correct.text: Reschedule so Task G starts before or at the same time as Task H.
correct.why: In start-to-start, the successor cannot start before the predecessor.

correct.text: Adjust dates so predecessor G starts no later than successor H starts.
correct.why: SS requires the predecessor to start first or together.

correct.text: Move the starts so G begins before or concurrent with H.
correct.why: Start-to-start forbids the successor starting earlier alone.

distractors.text: Leave Task H starting first and shift Task G to follow it.
distractors.trap: Violates the start-to-start logic.

distractors.text: Start both tasks together and accept the mismatch.
distractors.trap: Ignores that H currently precedes G.

distractors.text: Swap the labels so H becomes the predecessor G.
distractors.trap: Renaming changes the defined sequence arbitrarily.

distractors.text: Convert the link to finish-to-start without reviewing the work.
distractors.trap: Changes type instead of enforcing defined SS logic.

distractors.text: Add lag so H can keep starting earlier than G.
distractors.trap: Lag does not justify reversing SS start order.

distractors.text: Shorten Task H so the early start no longer matters.
distractors.trap: Duration change does not fix start-order violation.

explanation: Planning performance domain: a start-to-start dependency requires the predecessor to start no later than the successor.

prompts: On a data center relocation schedule, three dependencies are flagged: server decommissioning before physical transport, cable management after rack installation, and firewall configuration after network testing (per the security team's standard procedures). The sponsor wants to cut five days. Which dependency offers the least flexibility for schedule adjustment?

A data center move lists: decommission servers before transport; manage cables after racks go in; configure firewalls after network testing per security procedures. The sponsor seeks five days of compression. Which dependency is least flexible?

Relocating a data center, you track three links: servers must be decommissioned before transport; cabling follows rack install by preference; firewall work follows network tests per security standards. To cut five days, which dependency is least flexible?

For a data center relocation, dependencies include mandatory decommission-before-transport, preference-based cable work after racks, and procedure-based firewall config after network testing. Which offers the least schedule flexibility when cutting five days?

correct.text: Server decommissioning before physical transport, a mandatory dependency inherent to the work.
correct.why: Mandatory hard logic reflects physical necessity and resists reordering.

correct.text: Decommissioning servers before transport—the hard, inherent dependency in the work.
correct.why: Mandatory dependencies cannot be reordered to compress the schedule.

correct.text: The decommission-before-transport link, because it is a mandatory physical constraint.
correct.why: Hard dependencies are the least flexible for schedule compression.

distractors.text: Firewall configuration after network testing, set by security procedures.
distractors.trap: Procedure-based sequencing is discretionary and negotiable.

distractors.text: Cable management after rack installation, based on team preference.
distractors.trap: Preference-based sequencing is discretionary, not fixed.

distractors.text: All three are equally flexible since they were documented together.
distractors.trap: When documented does not change dependency type.

distractors.text: None of them can change because the sponsor requested a cut.
distractors.trap: Sponsor pressure does not make all links mandatory.

distractors.text: Only external vendor lead times, which are not among the three listed.
distractors.trap: Question asks among the three flagged dependencies.

distractors.text: Firewall work, because security rules always override physical constraints.
distractors.trap: Policy is often discretionary; physical necessity is hard.

explanation: Planning performance domain: mandatory (hard-logic) dependencies are inherent to the work and least adjustable, unlike discretionary or external ones.

prompts: Landscaping is scheduled to begin only after building construction finishes (a finish-to-start link). To meet an approaching deadline, you want landscaping to start while construction is still underway. Which dependency adjustment achieves this?
Construction and landscaping are finish-to-start today. You need landscaping to begin before construction fully ends to hit the deadline. Which adjustment creates that overlap?
A finish-to-start dependency keeps landscaping after construction completes. To start landscaping while building work continues, which dependency change is appropriate?
Landscaping currently waits on construction finish via finish-to-start. To advance the date by overlapping the two, what should you change?

correct.text: Add a lead to the finish-to-start dependency so landscaping starts early.
correct.why: A lead accelerates the successor before the predecessor finishes.

correct.text: Apply lead time on the finish-to-start link to let landscaping begin early.
correct.why: Lead creates intentional overlap on a finish-to-start link.

correct.text: Introduce a lead on the FS relationship so landscaping can start sooner.
correct.why: Lead advances the successor while keeping the dependency type.

distractors.text: Remove the dependency between construction and landscaping.
distractors.trap: Deletes needed logic instead of overlapping work.

distractors.text: Fast-track construction so landscaping can start sooner.
distractors.trap: Speeds the predecessor but may not overlap the two.

distractors.text: Change it to start-to-start with a lag.
distractors.trap: Adding lag delays rather than advances the successor.

distractors.text: Add lag to the finish-to-start link so dates compress.
distractors.trap: Lag delays the successor; it does not create overlap.

distractors.text: Convert both activities to milestones with no duration.
distractors.trap: Milestones hide work and do not create overlap.

distractors.text: Force landscaping finish to equal construction start.
distractors.trap: Wrong relationship and ignores needed sequencing.

explanation: Planning performance domain: a lead intentionally overlaps dependent activities to compress the schedule.

prompts: Reviewing a Gantt chart, you see 'Market Research Analysis' (Task A) has 8 days of total float while 'Competitive Benchmarking' (Task B) has 0. Both feed the 'Strategy Development' milestone. A team member asks to extend Task A by 10 days. What is the best response?

correct.text: Explain that 10 days exceeds Task A's float by 2, so the milestone would slip without compression.
correct.why: Consuming all 8 days of float plus 2 more pushes the milestone, requiring crashing or fast-tracking.

distractors.text: Extend Task B instead, since its zero float means it needs more time.
distractors.trap: Zero float means it's critical and can't be extended at all.

distractors.text: Approve it, since Task A has float and the analysis adds value.
distractors.trap: Ignores that 10 days exceeds the 8 days of available float.

distractors.text: Split the 10 days across A and B based on their float.
distractors.trap: Task B has no float to allocate.

explanation: Planning performance domain: total float shows how much an activity can slip before the milestone moves; exceeding it forces schedule compression.

prompts: In critical path scheduling an activity has an early start of day 5, a duration of 10 days, and a late start of day 15. What is its total float?

correct.text: 10 days.
correct.why: Total float equals late start minus early start: 15 - 5 = 10 days.

distractors.text: 0 days.
distractors.trap: Would mean the activity is critical, but LS exceeds ES here.

distractors.text: 5 days.
distractors.trap: Miscalculates the LS - ES difference.

distractors.text: 15 days.
distractors.trap: Uses the late start value alone, not the difference.

explanation: Planning performance domain: total float = LS - ES (or LF - EF), the slack before the project end date is affected.

prompts: A 16-week data center migration must now finish in 12 weeks. The critical path runs sequentially through hardware installation, network configuration, and system testing. A contingency fund can bring in specialized contractors, and specialists say network configuration could start before all hardware installation completes. Which compression combination should you recommend?

correct.text: Crash hardware installation with contractors and carefully fast-track it with network configuration.
correct.why: Crashing the first critical activity plus controlled overlap attacks the timeline where it pays off most.

distractors.text: Fast-track all three phases at once with the current resources.
distractors.trap: Overlapping everything sharply raises coordination and rework risk.

distractors.text: Fast-track installation and configuration but hold the fund for quality issues.
distractors.trap: Leaves available crashing resources unused.

distractors.text: Crash system testing and fast-track configuration into testing only.
distractors.trap: Rushing testing raises quality risk and skips earlier gains.

explanation: Planning performance domain: combining crashing (added resources) and fast-tracking (overlap) compresses schedule while balancing cost and risk.

prompts: For a concrete-curing activity the team gives PERT estimates of 8 days optimistic, 12 most likely, and 22 pessimistic. What is the activity's standard deviation?

correct.text: 2.33 days, from (pessimistic - optimistic) / 6.
correct.why: PERT standard deviation is (P - O)/6 = (22 - 8)/6 = 2.33 days.

distractors.text: 4.67 days, from (pessimistic - optimistic) / 3.
distractors.trap: Uses the wrong divisor, doubling the result.

distractors.text: 3.50 days, from (pessimistic - most likely) / 4.
distractors.trap: Wrong estimates and wrong divisor.

distractors.text: 1.75 days, from the square root of a weighted-average variance.
distractors.trap: PERT SD isn't derived that way.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain: PERT standard deviation (P - O)/6 quantifies an activity's schedule variability.

prompts: You have PERT standard deviations for all activities on a path. How do you combine them to assess the uncertainty in the total project duration?
Given PERT standard deviations for each activity on a path, how should you aggregate them for path-duration uncertainty?
PERT analysis provides a standard deviation per path activity. How do you combine those to judge overall duration uncertainty?
To evaluate uncertainty of total path duration from activity PERT standard deviations, what is the correct aggregation method?

correct.text: Square each standard deviation to get variances and sum them.
correct.why: Variances are additive along a path; standard deviations are not.

correct.text: Convert each standard deviation to a variance by squaring, then add the variances.
correct.why: Path variance is the sum of activity variances under PERT.

correct.text: Square the activity standard deviations and add those variances together.
correct.why: Only variances sum; do not add standard deviations directly.

distractors.text: Sum the standard deviations directly.
distractors.trap: Standard deviations are not additive.

distractors.text: Set project SD equal to the sum of activity SDs.
distractors.trap: Overstates spread; only variances add.

distractors.text: Set project SD equal to the average of activity SDs.
distractors.trap: Averaging discards total variability.

distractors.text: Multiply all activity standard deviations together.
distractors.trap: Multiplication has no basis in PERT aggregation.

distractors.text: Use only the largest activity standard deviation as the path SD.
distractors.trap: Ignores cumulative uncertainty from other tasks.

distractors.text: Subtract the smallest SD from the largest SD for the path.
distractors.trap: Range of SDs is not path variance.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain: path duration uncertainty aggregates by summing activity variances, not standard deviations.

prompts: A 14-week retail POS rollout tied to Black Friday shows an SPI of 0.85 at week 8. The critical path runs database migration, then system integration, then staff training. The sponsor allows budget flexibility and moderate risk; the team notes training materials can be built during integration and database specialists are available to hire. Which strategy best balances recovery and risk?

correct.text: Crash database migration and develop training materials in parallel with integration.
correct.why: It crashes technical work where quality matters and overlaps only the low-risk, already-feasible work.

distractors.text: Fast-track database migration and integration, and add resources to training.
distractors.trap: Overlapping migration with integration is high-risk; integration needs a stable database.

distractors.text: Fast-track all three critical activities by overlapping each phase.
distractors.trap: Excessive overlap exceeds the sponsor's moderate-risk tolerance.

distractors.text: Crash system integration first because it's the longest phase.
distractors.trap: Longest is not the best crash target; specialists are available for migration.

explanation: Planning performance domain: tailoring compression, crashing where quality is critical and fast-tracking where overlap is proven, recovers schedule at acceptable risk.

prompts: Several activities have flexible start and finish dates within their float. How can you use that flexibility to level resource usage?
Multiple activities can slide within available float. How do you apply that flexibility to smooth resource demand?
When activities have float allowing flexible starts and finishes, how should you use it to level resources?
Activities on your schedule have usable float around their dates. How can that flexibility support resource leveling?

correct.text: Shift activity start and finish dates within their float to smooth resource demand.
correct.why: Rescheduling within float rebalances load without moving the end date.

correct.text: Move starts and finishes inside available float so resource peaks flatten.
correct.why: Float-based shifting levels demand without changing scope.

correct.text: Reschedule work within float windows to even out resource usage over time.
correct.why: Using float levels resources while protecting the finish date.

distractors.text: Add resources or outsource tasks to remove the conflicts.
distractors.trap: Adds cost instead of using existing float.

distractors.text: Reassign resources broadly to raise availability everywhere.
distractors.trap: Doesn't use scheduling flexibility; may overstaff.

distractors.text: Overlap sequential tasks to finish sooner.
distractors.trap: That's compression, not leveling.

distractors.text: Cut scope from noncritical work to free capacity.
distractors.trap: Changes deliverables rather than leveling dates.

distractors.text: Ignore float and crash every overallocated activity.
distractors.trap: Crashing costs money and skips float usage.

distractors.text: Freeze all dates and accept peak resource overload.
distractors.trap: Does nothing to level the resource profile.

explanation: Planning performance domain: resource leveling redistributes work within available float to balance demand without altering scope.

prompts: A new team member asks why iteration length stays constant even when the workload varies between sprints. Which answer best captures the principle behind fixed time-box lengths?
A newcomer wonders why sprint length does not change when work volume swings. Which reply best explains fixed timeboxes?
Someone new to the team asks why iterations keep the same duration despite uneven workloads. What principle justifies fixed lengths?
Why keep iteration length fixed when some sprints look heavier than others? Which answer best states the principle?

correct.text: Fixed durations create a predictable cadence that aids planning, stakeholder expectations, and steady improvement.
correct.why: A constant rhythm enables velocity, forecasting, and comparable retrospectives.

correct.text: Constant timeboxes give a reliable rhythm for planning, expectations, and continuous improvement.
correct.why: Predictable cadence supports forecasting and learning across iterations.

correct.text: Keeping length fixed builds a steady cadence for planning, stakeholder rhythm, and improvement.
correct.why: Stable boxes make velocity and retrospectives meaningful over time.

distractors.text: They let the team bank buffer time to reuse on harder features later.
distractors.trap: Timeboxes don't carry unused capacity forward.

distractors.text: They ensure every team member completes equal work each iteration.
distractors.trap: Agile expects varying individual contribution.

distractors.text: They automatically adjust team capacity from past completion rates.
distractors.trap: Capacity planning is separate from fixed length.

distractors.text: They guarantee that all backlog items will finish every sprint.
distractors.trap: Fixed length does not force full backlog completion.

distractors.text: They eliminate the need for prioritization within the iteration.
distractors.trap: Prioritization remains essential inside every timebox.

distractors.text: They replace the need for definition of done and quality checks.
distractors.trap: Cadence does not substitute for quality agreements.

explanation: Development Approach and Life Cycle domain: fixed timeboxes provide the cadence and predictability central to iterative delivery.

prompts: On a fast-moving software build, minor delays are gradually accumulating and the schedule is slipping. You want to monitor schedule performance and catch variances promptly using your scheduling tool. Which feature should you use?
Small slips are stacking up on a rapid software delivery. You need the scheduling tool to track schedule performance and surface variances early. Which capability should you use?
A software build is losing time in small increments. To monitor schedule performance and detect variances quickly in the schedule tool, what should you rely on?
Minor delays are compounding on a fast software project. Which scheduling-tool feature best tracks schedule performance and flags variance promptly?

correct.text: Earned value management features to track schedule variance.
correct.why: EVM quantifies schedule variance and indices for early detection.

correct.text: Earned value analysis functions that report schedule variance.
correct.why: EVM integrates scope and schedule to measure performance gaps.

correct.text: Schedule variance tracking through earned value management metrics.
correct.why: EVM metrics highlight schedule drift before it becomes severe.

distractors.text: Reporting tools that generate progress reports for stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Communicates status but doesn't quantify variance.

distractors.text: Automated alerts that notify the team of deadlines.
distractors.trap: Reminds of dates without measuring performance.

distractors.text: Critical path analysis to identify essential tasks.
distractors.trap: Finds the longest path, not ongoing variance.

distractors.text: Resource histograms that show weekly staffing levels.
distractors.trap: Shows capacity, not schedule performance variance.

distractors.text: Milestone checklists that mark deliverables as complete.
distractors.trap: Binary status lacks integrated variance measurement.

distractors.text: Chat integrations that broadcast daily stand-up notes.
distractors.trap: Communication aid, not schedule variance analysis.

explanation: Measurement performance domain: earned value management quantifies schedule variance for timely corrective action.

prompts: Implementing Critical Chain Project Management, you notice team members routinely pad individual task estimates with safety margins. What does CCPM recommend for handling this embedded safety?
While rolling out CCPM, you see people padding every task with personal safety time. How should that embedded safety be handled?
Under Critical Chain Project Management, individual estimates include hidden safety. What does CCPM advise doing with that padding?
Team members habitually add safety to each task estimate as you adopt CCPM. What is the recommended treatment of that safety?

correct.text: Remove safety from individual tasks and pool it into shared buffers at key points.
correct.why: Aggregated buffers counter student syndrome and protect the chain.

correct.text: Strip task-level padding and place the safety into shared project and feeding buffers.
correct.why: Pooling safety improves focus and statistical chain protection.

correct.text: Eliminate embedded task safety and concentrate it in strategically placed buffers.
correct.why: Shared buffers manage uncertainty better than local padding.

distractors.text: Cut each estimate by a fixed percentage and spread the time evenly.
distractors.trap: Arbitrary and ignores strategic buffer placement.

distractors.text: Keep task safety and add extra project buffers on top.
distractors.trap: Double-pads and lengthens the schedule.

distractors.text: Keep safety on high-risk tasks and pool only the low-risk ones.
distractors.trap: CCPM removes safety from all tasks, not selectively.

distractors.text: Hide safety only in management reserve outside the network.
distractors.trap: CCPM uses chain buffers, not only management reserve.

distractors.text: Leave padding in place so no one feels schedule pressure.
distractors.trap: Preserves student syndrome and Parkinson's Law effects.

distractors.text: Move all safety into a single buffer after every single task.
distractors.trap: Per-task buffers recreate local padding, not aggregation.

explanation: Planning performance domain: CCPM strips individual safety and consolidates it into project and feeding buffers for statistical protection.

prompts: A Gantt chart shows two creative tasks overlapping next week, both needing the same designer, creating a three-day resource over-allocation. The second task has float, so the finish date isn't threatened. Which adjustment best resolves the conflict?
Next week two design tasks overlap and both need the same designer for three days of over-allocation. The later task has float and the project end date is safe. What best fixes it?
Two creative activities share one designer next week, causing a three-day overallocation. The second task has available float. Which adjustment is best?
Your Gantt has two concurrent creative tasks competing for one designer (three-day overallocation). Float exists on the second task. How should you resolve it?

correct.text: Use resource leveling to delay the second task into its available float.
correct.why: Leveling into float clears overallocation without moving the end date.

correct.text: Level resources by shifting the second task later within its float.
correct.why: Using float removes the conflict while protecting the baseline finish.

correct.text: Apply resource leveling so the second task slides into its free float.
correct.why: Float absorbs the delay and the critical path stays intact.

distractors.text: Insert a contingency milestone and rebaseline the whole schedule.
distractors.trap: Hides the issue and creates needless change-control work.

distractors.text: Add a lead so the second task starts two days earlier.
distractors.trap: Still overlaps the designer and may create conflicts.

distractors.text: Split the first task and give half to freelancers.
distractors.trap: Adds cost when float already can absorb the delay.

distractors.text: Crash both tasks with overtime for the shared designer.
distractors.trap: Extra cost is unnecessary when float is available.

distractors.text: Cancel the second task and reopen it after the release.
distractors.trap: Drops scope instead of leveling within float.

distractors.text: Mark the designer as 200% available for that week only.
distractors.trap: Falsifies capacity rather than resolving the conflict.

explanation: Planning performance domain: resource leveling shifts a non-critical activity into its float to resolve over-allocation without disrupting the baseline.

prompts: Assembly can begin only after parts manufacturing finishes, but a safety inspection taking up to two days must occur in between. How should this be modeled in a precedence diagram?

correct.text: Finish-to-start with a lag of up to two days for the inspection.
correct.why: A lag inserts the required waiting time after the predecessor finishes and before the successor starts.

distractors.text: Start-to-start with a two-day lead between the activities.
distractors.trap: SS lets both begin together, which the sequence forbids.

distractors.text: Finish-to-start with a lead of -2 days before manufacturing finishes.
distractors.trap: A lead pulls the successor earlier, not later.

distractors.text: Start-to-finish with a two-day lag after manufacturing starts.
distractors.trap: SF links a start to a finish, not this case.

explanation: Planning performance domain: a lag on a finish-to-start dependency models required waiting time between activities.

topic: Selecting scheduling tooling for complex dependencies
prompts: On a large program of interconnected buildings with interdependent timelines and shared resources, your low-cost scheduling tool, chosen for simplicity, can't model the complex dependencies, causing conflicts and delays. How should you improve the scheduling process?

A multi-building program has intertwined schedules and shared resources, but your simple low-cost scheduler cannot model the complex dependencies, producing conflicts and slips. How should you improve scheduling?
Interdependent facility projects share resources, yet the inexpensive scheduling tool cannot handle advanced dependencies, creating delays. What is the best improvement?

Your program's low-cost schedule tool was picked for ease of use, but it cannot model complex cross-building dependencies and shared resources. Conflicts and delays result. How should you improve the process?

correct.text: Adopt more capable scheduling software that supports dynamic resource leveling and advanced dependencies.
correct.why: Matching the tool to complexity enables accurate constraints and planning.

correct.text: Move to scheduling software that models advanced dependencies and dynamic resource leveling.
correct.why: Adequate tooling captures interdependencies and shared-resource limits.

correct.text: Upgrade to a scheduler with advanced dependency modeling and resource-leveling capability.
correct.why: Tool capability must fit program complexity for reliable plans.

distractors.text: Keep the tool but hold more meetings to adjust the schedule manually.
distractors.trap: Labor-intensive and error-prone; ignores the root cause.

distractors.text: Switch to spreadsheets to map dependencies by hand.
distractors.trap: Still lacks leveling and dependency modeling.

distractors.text: Delegate scheduling to experienced members to work around the tool.
distractors.trap: Doesn't fix the tool's inability to model complexity.

distractors.text: Reduce the program to independent projects so the simple tool fits.
distractors.trap: Artificial isolation ignores real shared constraints.

distractors.text: Freeze all dependency changes and accept the resulting conflicts.
distractors.trap: Stops improving the model instead of enabling it.

distractors.text: Replace the schedule with a high-level milestone list only.
distractors.trap: Loses the detail needed for complex interdependencies.

explanation: Tailoring principle: tools and techniques should be scaled to the project's complexity rather than oversimplifying it.

prompts: During planning you find that a piece of highly specialized equipment is over-scheduled, threatening delays. Which resource leveling technique best addresses this while protecting deadlines?

While building the schedule, one highly specialized machine is booked on more work than it can handle, putting milestones at risk. How should you level resources to address this without needlessly missing deadlines?
A manufacturing project over-allocates a scarce specialized tool across concurrent tasks, creating schedule risk. Which leveling approach best resolves the conflict while guarding due dates?
Planning reveals a unique piece of equipment is double-booked across activities. Which resource-leveling response best eases the over-allocation while still protecting deadlines?

correct.text: Delay dependent tasks to match the equipment availability.
correct.why: Leveling around the constrained resource resolves conflict without changing scope.

correct.text: Reschedule dependent work to align with when the equipment is free.
correct.why: Shifting tasks to resource availability clears over-allocation without cutting scope.

correct.text: Move dependent activities later to fit the specialized equipment calendar.
correct.why: Scheduling around the scarce resource removes contention while protecting deliverables.

distractors.text: Assign alternative equipment to each of the conflicting scheduled tasks.
distractors.trap: Specialized equipment usually has no true substitute.

distractors.text: Rework task dependencies extensively in hope of reducing resource contention.
distractors.trap: Does not directly clear the over-allocation.

distractors.text: Pull additional matching equipment from other projects without checking impact.
distractors.trap: May be unavailable and disrupts other work.

distractors.text: Shorten each task that uses the equipment so the calendar looks feasible.
distractors.trap: Cutting duration does not create extra capacity.

distractors.text: Fast-track all equipment-dependent activities to clear the overload sooner.
distractors.trap: Parallelism worsens contention on scarce gear.

distractors.text: Ignore the planning conflict until execution exposes the bottleneck on site.
distractors.trap: Defers a known planning conflict into delivery.

explanation: Planning performance domain: resource leveling reschedules work to the availability of a constrained resource without altering scope.

prompts: After applying resource-constrained scheduling, the project duration extends beyond the original baseline even though all activities are correctly sequenced by resource availability. Which characteristic explains this?
Resource-constrained scheduling lengthens overall project duration past the original baseline, though activities follow resource availability correctly. What explains this result?
You apply resource-limited scheduling and the finish date slips even with correct resource-based sequencing. Which characteristic accounts for the longer duration?
After leveling to resource limits, the schedule is longer than the logic-only baseline despite valid sequencing. What explains the extension?

correct.text: Limited resources force activities to start later than earliest logic dates, lengthening the project.
correct.why: Resource limits add constraints beyond logic, delaying starts and extending duration.

correct.text: Scarce resources push starts past earliest dates allowed by logic alone, stretching duration.
correct.why: Availability constraints delay work beyond pure network logic and lengthen the schedule.

correct.text: Capacity limits delay activity starts beyond logic-only early dates, extending overall duration.
correct.why: Resource constraints serialize work and force later starts than dependencies alone require.

distractors.text: The critical path stays fixed because logic always outranks resource availability in scheduling.
distractors.trap: A resource-critical path can emerge and change it.

distractors.text: Resource constraints compress the schedule by forcing more parallel execution of activities.
distractors.trap: Shared resources force serial, not parallel, work.

distractors.text: Leveling algorithms automatically shorten task durations to offset the new sequencing rules.
distractors.trap: Leveling moves work; it does not shorten durations.

distractors.text: Resource limits remove float so every path becomes critical equally across the network.
distractors.trap: Not every path becomes critical; duration extends from delays.

distractors.text: Activity durations inflate automatically to absorb resource wait time built into the model.
distractors.trap: Duration of tasks is not lengthened; starts are delayed.

distractors.text: The baseline shortens because resource sequencing eliminates idle gaps between activities.
distractors.trap: Opposite effect: constrained scheduling typically extends finish.

explanation: Planning performance domain: resource-constrained scheduling delays activities to resource availability, typically extending duration versus a time-only baseline.

prompts: On a highway project, asphalt paving can begin once 42.5% of road grading is done; line striping must start exactly two days after paving begins; and guardrail installation must finish at the same moment striping finishes. Which PDM configuration represents these constraints?

correct.text: SS+42.5% (grading to paving), SS+2 days (paving to striping), FF (striping to guardrail).
correct.why: Percentage-based SS models partial-completion start, SS+lag the timed start, and FF the simultaneous finish.

distractors.text: FS+42.5% lag (grading to paving), SS+2 (paving to striping), FS (striping to guardrail).
distractors.trap: FS waits for grading to fully finish, not 42.5%.

distractors.text: SS+lag (grading to paving), SS+2 (paving to striping), SF (guardrail to striping).
distractors.trap: SF doesn't force both to finish together.

distractors.text: SS+42.5% (grading to paving), FS+2 (paving to striping), FF+2 (striping to guardrail).
distractors.trap: FS+2 starts striping after paving finishes, not begins.

explanation: Planning performance domain: PDM relationships (SS with lead/lag, FF) precisely encode overlap and simultaneous-finish constraints.

prompts: A software project on time-boxed scheduling struggles with estimation—some teams overestimate and others underestimate—causing inefficient allocation and disruptions. Which approach best fits time-boxed principles to improve estimating?
Teams on a time-boxed software effort produce mixed estimates: some pad heavily, others under-call effort, which wastes capacity. What approach best improves estimating under timeboxing?
A product group using fixed-length timeboxes has inconsistent estimates that skew allocation and create mid-cycle churn. How should they improve estimating while honoring timebox principles?
Estimation accuracy varies widely across squads on a time-boxed program, harming planning. Which action best fits timeboxed delivery to improve future estimates?

correct.text: Hold a retrospective each timebox to review estimate accuracy and adjust next estimates.
correct.why: Inspecting actuals versus estimates each cycle drives continuous improvement inside fixed timeboxes.

correct.text: Compare planned versus actual effort after each timebox and refine future estimates.
correct.why: Empirical review each cycle tightens estimates without abandoning fixed-length timeboxes.

correct.text: Inspect estimate accuracy after every timebox and update forecasting for next cycle.
correct.why: Cadenced inspection of estimate error improves accuracy while preserving timebox discipline.

distractors.text: Add a mandatory end-of-timebox buffer so any carried-over work can always finish.
distractors.trap: Undermines fixed-length timeboxes and rewards padding.

distractors.text: Replace time-boxing with a fully flexible model that drops fixed cycle lengths entirely.
distractors.trap: Abandons the framework instead of improving estimating.

distractors.text: Extend every timebox by a fixed percentage of duration as a permanent safety margin.
distractors.trap: Breaks fixed duration and hides root causes.

distractors.text: Double all story points across the board so underestimates never threaten the timebox.
distractors.trap: Systematic padding masks accuracy problems.

distractors.text: Freeze estimates after sprint planning and never revisit them based on delivery results.
distractors.trap: Blocks learning from actual delivery performance.

distractors.text: Assign estimating only to managers outside the delivery team for greater consistency.
distractors.trap: Removes empirical team ownership of estimate quality.

explanation: Development Approach and Life Cycle domain: retrospectives use empirical data to improve estimation while preserving the timebox cadence.

prompts: Using Critical Chain Project Management, you find during planning that one specialized resource is needed for two critical tasks. How should you handle this?
In a CCPM schedule, the same specialized person is required on two critical-chain activities. What is the appropriate planning response?
While building a critical-chain plan, you discover one scarce specialist must support two critical tasks. How should you address this?
Critical Chain Project Management reveals a single specialized resource is booked for two critical tasks. What should you do?

correct.text: Buffer the resource between the tasks and identify potential alternatives.
correct.why: Resource buffers protect the chain from contention; alternatives reduce single-point risk.

correct.text: Place a resource buffer between the tasks and prepare backup resource options.
correct.why: CCPM uses resource buffers for contention and seeks alternatives to cut dependency.

correct.text: Protect the chain with resource buffering and list substitute resource options.
correct.why: Buffering capacity between contending tasks and finding alternatives safeguards the chain.

distractors.text: Let the specialized resource multitask across both critical tasks at once.
distractors.trap: CCPM discourages multitasking; it causes delays.

distractors.text: Stretch other tasks durations to absorb any delay from resource contention.
distractors.trap: Does not address resource contention directly.

distractors.text: Ignore the shared-resource conflict; it will resolve as the project proceeds.
distractors.trap: Invites bottlenecks and schedule slippage.

distractors.text: Remove both tasks from the critical chain so contention no longer matters.
distractors.trap: Avoids the constraint instead of protecting the chain.

distractors.text: Add student-syndrome padding to every noncritical task across the network.
distractors.trap: Misuses padding and ignores the shared resource.

distractors.text: Force both critical tasks to start on the same day to finish earlier overall.
distractors.trap: Maximizes contention rather than buffering it.

explanation: Planning performance domain: CCPM uses resource buffers, not multitasking, to protect the critical chain from resource contention.

prompts: On a building project, structural work can start only after the foundation is complete, while facade work can start once 81% of structural work is done. What precedence relationships describe this?

correct.text: Foundation to structural is finish-to-start; structural to facade is start-to-start.
correct.why: Structural waits for the foundation to finish, but facade overlaps structural after a partial-completion start.

distractors.text: All relationships are finish-to-start.
distractors.trap: Ignores the structural-facade overlap.

distractors.text: Foundation to structural is finish-to-finish; structural to facade is start-to-start.
distractors.trap: Structural can't start until the foundation fully finishes.

distractors.text: Foundation to structural is start-to-start; structural to facade is finish-to-finish.
distractors.trap: Structural needs the foundation complete first.

explanation: Planning performance domain: finish-to-start and start-to-start (with lag) relationships model sequential versus overlapping work.

prompts: On a CCPM telecom project at week eight, the fever chart shows 58.5% of the project buffer consumed while 63.5% of critical chain tasks are complete. A key engineer for an upcoming critical task requests three days off, with no equivalent backup. Which response shows sound CCPM judgment?

correct.text: Approve the leave, since buffer use trails progress, and arrange knowledge transfer to ready a backup.
correct.why: Buffer consumed (58.5%) is below work done (63.5%), so the project is healthy and can absorb the variation.

distractors.text: Approve the leave and add three days to the project buffer.
distractors.trap: CCPM doesn't arbitrarily resize buffers during execution.

distractors.text: Deny the leave to avoid pushing the buffer into the red zone.
distractors.trap: Overly rigid given a healthy buffer status.

distractors.text: Escalate to the sponsor and start compressing later tasks.
distractors.trap: Overreaction for a routine decision on a healthy project.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain plus the Team principle: buffers exist to absorb variation, enabling supportive decisions when the fever chart is green.

prompts: On an agile project, the team underestimated a task's complexity, causing a schedule slip. As project manager, how should you adjust the schedule?
An agile team finds a backlog item far more complex than planned, and the iteration schedule slips. How should you adjust the plan?
Mid-sprint, complexity of a story exceeds the original estimate and delivery is late. As PM, how should you update the schedule?
After underestimating work complexity on an agile delivery, the team is behind. What is the best schedule adjustment?

correct.text: Re-estimate remaining work using what you learned and adjust the plan.
correct.why: Agile inspects actuals and adapts estimates as understanding improves.

correct.text: Update remaining estimates with new insight and revise the schedule.
correct.why: Empirical re-estimation keeps the plan honest after complexity was misjudged.

correct.text: Recalculate unfinished effort from current knowledge and replan timelines.
correct.why: Adapting estimates to learned complexity is core agile schedule management.

distractors.text: Have the team work overtime until the original schedule is recovered.
distractors.trap: Unsustainable and erodes a steady pace.

distractors.text: Reassign the task to a more experienced member without re-estimating.
distractors.trap: Does not fix the underlying estimation gap.

distractors.text: Remove the task from scope entirely so the original date is protected.
distractors.trap: Drops needed value solely to hit a date.

distractors.text: Keep the original estimate and hope velocity will catch up next sprint.
distractors.trap: Ignores known complexity and leaves the plan false.

distractors.text: Add a permanent safety factor to every future story regardless of size.
distractors.trap: Blind padding hides learning instead of re-estimating.

distractors.text: Freeze the backlog so no further estimation changes can be made later.
distractors.trap: Blocks adaptation after new information appears.

explanation: Development Approach and Life Cycle domain: agile teams adapt plans by re-estimating from empirical results.

prompts: One qualified technician must calibrate three tower sites, working one site at a time, starting day 3. Site Alpha (5 days) precedes a day-18 activation milestone; Site Beta (3 days) has 7 days of float and no external dependency; Site Gamma (4 days) must finish before a day-14 regulatory inspection. The operations director wants Beta done first. How should you sequence the work?

correct.text: Gamma first from day 3, then Alpha from day 7, then Beta using its float.
correct.why: Fixed external constraints (Gamma's day-14 deadline, Alpha's milestone) outrank a preference and Beta's slack.

distractors.text: Beta first as the director requested, then Alpha, then Gamma on remaining float.
distractors.trap: Preference over hard deadlines risks missing Gamma's inspection.

distractors.text: Sequence by ascending float, starting with Alpha as least flexible.
distractors.trap: Gamma's earlier deadline makes it the true priority.

distractors.text: Alpha first since it precedes the milestone, then Gamma, then Beta.
distractors.trap: Gamma's day-14 deadline is more urgent than Alpha's day-18.

explanation: Planning performance domain: resource-constrained sequencing prioritizes fixed external constraints and least float over stakeholder preference.

topic: Prioritizing a constrained resource via impact analysis
prompts: Near a hard deadline, a critical issue needs your only senior developer, who is fully committed to another equally important task. The sponsor demands on-time delivery. How should you use this constrained resource?

With a firm deadline approaching, only one senior developer can fix a critical issue, but that person is fully assigned to another equally important task. The sponsor insists on on-time delivery. How should you allocate them?

A hard end date looms and a critical defect requires your sole senior engineer, who is already fully booked on another equally vital task. Leadership demands the date. How do you deploy this scarce person?

Your only senior developer is fully committed to one high-priority task when a second critical issue also needs them near a hard deadline. The sponsor wants on-time delivery. How should you use the resource?

correct.text: Analyze the impact of delaying each task, assign the developer to the higher-priority one, and reset expectations with the sponsor.
correct.why: Data-driven prioritization plus transparent communication protects overall project value.

correct.text: Compare delay impacts, put the senior on the higher-priority work, and realign sponsor expectations.
correct.why: Impact analysis and clear stakeholder updates guide scarce-resource trade-offs.

correct.text: Assess which delay hurts more, assign the senior accordingly, and communicate the decision to the sponsor.
correct.why: Prioritize by impact and keep the sponsor informed when capacity is finite.

distractors.text: Bring in a qualified external consultant to handle the critical issue, freeing the senior developer to stay on the original committed task.
distractors.trap: Onboarding time and budget may still miss the deadline.

distractors.text: Split the senior developer's time equally between both tasks so neither falls fully behind as the deadline approaches.
distractors.trap: Context-switching slows both under hard-deadline urgency.

distractors.text: Assign the critical issue to a junior developer and have the senior provide remote guidance while remaining on the original task.
distractors.trap: Risks slow or flawed resolution without hands-on expertise.

distractors.text: Keep the senior on the original task and quietly defer the critical issue until after the deadline.
distractors.trap: Ignores impact analysis and surprises stakeholders.

distractors.text: Ask both product owners to wait while the senior multitasks at half pace on each.
distractors.trap: Half-effort multitasking rarely meets hard deadlines.

distractors.text: Cancel the lower-visibility task without impact analysis or sponsor discussion.
distractors.trap: Skips prioritization data and stakeholder alignment.

explanation: Stakeholder engagement plus Delivery domain: assess impact, prioritize by value, and align expectations before committing a scarce resource.

prompts: A marketing team on one-week timeboxes committed to two video assets and three static graphics. Mid-iteration they learn the video work needs equipment that won't arrive until the next iteration; the graphics are nearly done. The director expects everything by iteration end. How should you handle this while keeping time-boxed integrity?

In a one-week timebox, marketing planned two videos and three static graphics. Videos are blocked on equipment arriving next iteration, while graphics are almost finished. Leadership still expects full delivery this cycle. How do you protect timebox integrity?

A content team works in one-week timeboxes with two video deliverables and three graphics committed. Mid-cycle, video gear is delayed until the next iteration; graphics are nearly complete. The director wants everything now. What preserves the timebox?

Mid one-week iteration, two committed videos need equipment unavailable until next iteration, while three static graphics are nearly done. Stakeholders expect all five items by timebox end. How should you respond?

correct.text: Finish graphics this timebox, defer videos to a later iteration, and communicate the plan.
correct.why: Timebox length stays fixed; blocked work is deferred and stakeholders stay informed.

correct.text: Complete near-finished graphics now, move blocked videos later, and share the update.
correct.why: Fixed timeboxes deliver ready work; blocked items move forward with clear communication.

correct.text: Ship ready graphics this cycle, park blocked videos, and reset stakeholder expectations.
correct.why: Preserves fixed duration by finishing unblocked value and deferring blocked work.

distractors.text: Attempt the videos with whatever equipment is on hand and plan quality rework later.
distractors.trap: Delivers low-quality output and wasteful rework.

distractors.text: Extend the timebox by several days so all five committed items can still be finished.
distractors.trap: Violates the fixed-duration principle.

distractors.text: Cut nearly finished graphics scope to free effort for both content types this cycle.
distractors.trap: Reduces near-finished value for no gain on blocked work.

distractors.text: Cancel the entire iteration and restart only after all required equipment has arrived.
distractors.trap: Wastes progress on work that can finish now.

distractors.text: Mark all five items done in the review ceremony and finish remaining quality afterward.
distractors.trap: Falsifies completion and breaks timebox honesty.

distractors.text: Pull the team off graphics to wait idle until the video production equipment arrives.
distractors.trap: Idles capacity instead of finishing ready work.

explanation: Development Approach and Life Cycle domain: timeboxes stay fixed in length; scope flexes and blocked work is deferred, not the schedule.

prompts: A key resource is required for several concurrent activities but is available only limited hours per day. To resolve the over-allocation without changing scope, which technique should you apply?
One critical person is needed on multiple overlapping activities yet has only limited daily hours. Scope must stay fixed. Which technique resolves the over-allocation?
A scarce specialist is over-allocated across concurrent tasks with fixed daily capacity. You cannot change scope. Which scheduling technique should you use?
Several concurrent activities demand the same key resource beyond available hours each day. Without changing scope, which technique fixes the over-allocation?

correct.text: Resource leveling, adjusting the schedule to the resource constraints.
correct.why: Leveling reschedules work to resource limits, even if duration extends.

correct.text: Resource leveling to reschedule work within the resource limited hours.
correct.why: Leveling resolves over-allocation by fitting work to real capacity.

correct.text: Apply resource leveling so timing respects the constrained resource hours.
correct.why: Schedule yields to resource limits to clear over-allocation without scope change.

distractors.text: Resource smoothing within available float while keeping dates fixed overall.
distractors.trap: Smoothing cannot resolve conflicts beyond float.

distractors.text: Crashing by adding resources to critical activities to protect the finish date.
distractors.trap: Compression technique, not a resource-constraint fix.

distractors.text: Fast-tracking by running more tasks in parallel to free the key resource.
distractors.trap: Compresses time and worsens resource conflict.

distractors.text: Scope reduction to eliminate concurrent demand on the over-allocated person.
distractors.trap: Changes scope rather than leveling the resource.

distractors.text: Monte Carlo analysis of activity durations without changing resource assignments.
distractors.trap: Risk modeling does not reallocate the resource.

distractors.text: Critical chain buffering on paths without moving any over-allocated task dates.
distractors.trap: Buffers alone leave concurrent over-allocation intact.

explanation: Planning performance domain: resource leveling resolves over-allocation by rescheduling to resource limits, unlike smoothing or compression.

prompts: A project has six activities with these durations (days) and dependencies: A(2, no predecessor), B(3, none), C(2, after A), D(2, after B), E(3, after C and D), F(1, after E). Only one resource is available at any time, so no two activities can run concurrently. What is the minimum duration to complete the project?

correct.text: 13 days
correct.why: With one shared resource everything runs serially, so the minimum equals the sum of all durations (2+3+2+2+3+1).

distractors.text: 12 days
distractors.trap: Assumes some activities overlap, ignoring the single-resource constraint.

distractors.text: 11 days
distractors.trap: Uses a parallel critical-path calculation instead of full serialization.

distractors.text: 14 days
distractors.trap: Adds a phantom buffer day not supported by the data.

explanation: Illustrates resource-constrained scheduling in the Schedule performance domain: when a single resource is shared, resource leveling forces serial execution and duration equals the sum of activity durations.

prompts: On a medical-device project, initial testing shows a cross-functional component fails several critical performance criteria, threatening regulatory milestones. Engineering, biomedical specialists, and a supplier all contributed. What should you do first?

A regulated medical-device deliverable fails critical performance tests; engineering, biomedical experts, and a supplier all worked on the component. Regulatory milestones are at risk. What is the first action?
Cross-functional testing on a medical device finds a shared component missing several critical criteria, endangering regulatory dates. Multiple parties contributed. What should you do first?
Early tests show a multi-party component on a medical-device project fails critical performance criteria and threatens regulatory milestones. What is your first step?

correct.text: Facilitate a joint review with involved parties to find root cause and agree corrective action.
correct.why: Joint root-cause diagnosis targets the real problem before more time or spend.

correct.text: Convene contributors to identify the root cause and agree corrective actions together.
correct.why: Cross-party analysis finds the true failure source before further effort.

correct.text: Lead a collaborative root-cause session with all parties and set a corrective plan.
correct.why: Shared diagnosis with responsible experts fixes causes, not just symptoms.

distractors.text: Extend the work package timeline to allow more testing cycles and design adjustments.
distractors.trap: Adds schedule without diagnosing why it failed.

distractors.text: Pull extra resources from other work to accelerate development and retesting effort.
distractors.trap: Throws resources at symptoms without root-cause insight.

distractors.text: Apply incremental trial-and-error changes until every critical performance criterion is met.
distractors.trap: Guesswork risks noncompliance and more rework.

distractors.text: Blame the supplier immediately and rebid the component without deeper failure analysis.
distractors.trap: Skips evidence and may miss the true cause.

distractors.text: Ship the component under a known-defect waiver and hope regulators still accept it.
distractors.trap: Waives critical criteria instead of fixing the defect.

distractors.text: Replace the entire subsystem without investigating how the failure mode actually occurred.
distractors.trap: Costly overreaction before understanding root cause.

explanation: Reflects the Quality performance domain: root-cause analysis on a defective deliverable must precede corrective action so effort addresses the actual cause.

prompts: You are drafting the scope statement for a CRM implementation after gathering needs from many departments and documenting objectives. Which element is most critical for establishing clear boundaries on what the project will deliver?
While writing the scope statement for a multi-department CRM rollout with objectives already captured, which element most critically sets clear delivery boundaries?
A CRM program has collected departmental needs and project objectives. Drafting the scope statement, what is most critical to establish clear boundaries on what will be delivered?
For a CRM implementation scope statement after needs gathering and objective definition, which element best establishes clear in-scope versus out-of-scope boundaries?

correct.text: Acceptance criteria and exclusions that define what is inside and outside the boundaries.
correct.why: Criteria and exclusions jointly set deliverable boundaries and limit scope creep.

correct.text: Clear acceptance criteria plus documented exclusions marking in-scope and out-of-scope work.
correct.why: Together they bound what is delivered and prevent misunderstanding.

correct.text: Defined acceptance criteria and exclusions that draw the project delivery boundaries.
correct.why: Boundary clarity comes from criteria of done and explicit exclusions.

distractors.text: A full list of stakeholder communication preferences, preferred channels, and meeting schedules.
distractors.trap: Belongs in communications planning, not scope boundaries.

distractors.text: Exclusions plus budget variance thresholds, management reserves, and contingency reserve amounts.
distractors.trap: Reserves and thresholds are cost items, not scope bounds.

distractors.text: Detailed resource assignments with individual work-hour commitments for every named project role.
distractors.trap: Resource planning does not define scope boundaries.

distractors.text: A complete RACI chart mapping every stakeholder decision right across the full program structure.
distractors.trap: Roles clarity is not the same as scope boundaries.

distractors.text: Vendor rate cards and preferred supplier lists without any deliverable boundary language included.
distractors.trap: Procurement data does not set deliverable boundaries.

distractors.text: A high-level vision slogan without measurable acceptance rules or explicit exclusion statements.
distractors.trap: Vision alone leaves boundaries ambiguous.

explanation: Reflects the Scope baseline (project scope statement): acceptance criteria and exclusions define the deliverable boundary.
